[20th](/wiki/List_of_North_American_cities_by_population "List of North American cities by population") in North America [8th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States [2nd](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_in_California_by_population "List of largest cities in California by population") in California

([/ˌsæn diˈeɪɡoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-san_diego.ogg "File:En-us-san diego.ogg") [*SAN dee-AY-goh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Spanish: [[san ˈdjeɣo]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) is a city on the [Pacific coast](/wiki/Pacific_coast "Pacific coast") of [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"), adjacent to the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"). It is the [eighth-most populous city in the U.S.](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") and [second-most populous city in California](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_in_California_by_population "List of largest cities in California by population") with a population of over 1.4 million, while the San Diego metropolitan area with over 3.3 million residents is the [18th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the country. San Diego is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County "San Diego County").[[17]](#cite_note-County_QuickFacts-17) It is known for its mild [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), extensive [beaches](/wiki/List_of_beaches_in_San_Diego_County "List of beaches in San Diego County") and [parks](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_San_Diego "List of parks in San Diego"), long association with the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"), and recent emergence as a [wireless](/wiki/Wireless "Wireless"), [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"), [healthcare](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_San_Diego "List of hospitals in San Diego"), and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") development center.

**San Diego** ([/ˌsæn diˈeɪɡoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-san_diego.ogg "File:En-us-san diego.ogg") [*SAN dee-AY-goh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Spanish: [[san ˈdjeɣo]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) is a city on the [Pacific coast](/wiki/Pacific_coast "Pacific coast") of [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"), adjacent to the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"). It is the [eighth-most populous city in the U.S.](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") and [second-most populous city in California](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_in_California_by_population "List of largest cities in California by population") with a population of over 1.4 million, while the San Diego metropolitan area with over 3.3 million residents is the [18th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the country. San Diego is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County "San Diego County").[[17]](#cite_note-County_QuickFacts-17) It is known for its mild [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), extensive [beaches](/wiki/List_of_beaches_in_San_Diego_County "List of beaches in San Diego County") and [parks](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_San_Diego "List of parks in San Diego"), long association with the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"), and recent emergence as a [wireless](/wiki/Wireless "Wireless"), [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"), [healthcare](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_San_Diego "List of hospitals in San Diego"), and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") development center.

Historically home to the [Kumeyaay](/wiki/Kumeyaay "Kumeyaay") people, San Diego has been referred to as the *Birthplace of California*, as it was the first site visited and settled by Europeans on what is now the [West Coast of the United States](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States").[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 1542, [Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo](/wiki/Juan_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Cabrillo "Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo") claimed the area for [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), forming the basis for the settlement of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California"), 200 years later. The [Presidio](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Diego "Presidio of San Diego") and [Mission San Diego de Alcalá](/wiki/Mission_San_Diego_de_Alcal%C3%A1 "Mission San Diego de Alcalá"), founded in 1769, formed the first European settlement in what is now California. In 1821, San Diego became part of the newly declared [Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire"). California was [ceded to the U.S.](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession") in 1848 following the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") and was [admitted as the 31st state](/wiki/California_Statehood_Act "California Statehood Act") in 1850.

The largest sectors of the [economy of San Diego](/wiki/Economy_of_San_Diego "Economy of San Diego") include military and defense-related activities, tourism, international trade, research, and manufacturing. The city is home to several universities, including [UC San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego"), [San Diego State University](/wiki/San_Diego_State_University "San Diego State University"), and the [University of San Diego](/wiki/University_of_San_Diego "University of San Diego"). San Diego is the economic center of the [San Diego–Tijuana](/wiki/San_Diego%E2%80%93Tijuana "San Diego–Tijuana") region, the second-most populous [transborder metropolitan area](/wiki/Transborder_agglomeration "Transborder agglomeration") in the Western Hemisphere, home to an estimated five million people as of 2022.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The primary border crossing between San Diego and [Tijuana](/wiki/Tijuana "Tijuana"), the [San Ysidro Port of Entry](/wiki/San_Ysidro_Port_of_Entry "San Ysidro Port of Entry"), is the busiest international land border crossing in the world outside of Asia ([fourth-busiest](/wiki/Border_checkpoint#Busiest_checkpoints_in_the_world "Border checkpoint") overall). [San Diego International Airport](/wiki/San_Diego_International_Airport "San Diego International Airport") (SAN) is the busiest single-runway airport in the United States.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

San Diego's name can be traced back to the 17th century when Spanish explorer [Sebastián Vizcaíno](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Vizca%C3%ADno "Sebastián Vizcaíno") bestowed it upon the area in 1602. He named the bay and the surrounding area "San Diego de Alcalá" in honor of [Saint Didacus of Alcalá](/wiki/Didacus_of_Alcal%C3%A1 "Didacus of Alcalá").[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21)

Prior to the Spanish establishment of San Diego, the [Kumeyaay](/wiki/Kumeyaay "Kumeyaay") town was called [Kosa'aay](/wiki/Kosa%27aay "Kosa'aay"), meaning "drying out place" in the [Kumeyaay language](/wiki/Kumeyaay_language "Kumeyaay language").[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22) After the establishment of San Diego, the Kumeyaay called town and city *Tepacul Watai*, meaning "Stacked Big".[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Luiseño](/wiki/Luise%C3%B1o_language "Luiseño language") speakers in the [North County region](/wiki/North_County_(San_Diego_area) "North County (San Diego area)") called it *Pushuyi*.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of San Diego](/wiki/History_of_San_Diego "History of San Diego")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of San Diego](/wiki/Timeline_of_San_Diego "Timeline of San Diego").

### Pre-colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-colonial period")]

[![Full length portrait of a man in his thirties wearing a long robe, woman and child visible behind him and dog to his left](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Kumeyaay_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Kumeyaay_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kumeyaay_(cropped).jpg)

The [Kumeyaay](/wiki/Kumeyaay_people "Kumeyaay people"), referred to by the Spanish as *Diegueños*, have inhabited the area for thousands of years.

What has been referred to as the [San Dieguito complex](/wiki/San_Dieguito_complex "San Dieguito complex") was established in the area at least 9,000 years ago.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The [Kumeyaay](/wiki/Kumeyaay "Kumeyaay") may have culturally evolved from this complex or migrated into the area around 1000 C.E.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Archaeologist [Malcolm Rogers](/wiki/Malcolm_Jennings_Rogers "Malcolm Jennings Rogers") hypothesized that the early cultures of San Diego were separate from the Kumeyaay, but this claim is disputed.[[27]](#cite_note-:3-27) Rogers later reevaluated his claims, yet they were influential in shaping historical tellings of early San Diego history.[[27]](#cite_note-:3-27)

The Kumeyaay established villages scattered across the region, including the village of [Kosa'aay](/wiki/Kosa%27aay "Kosa'aay") which was the Kumeyaay village that the future settlement of San Diego would stem from in today's [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town,_San_Diego "Old Town, San Diego").[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)[[28]](#cite_note-:2-28) The village of Kosa'aay was made up of thirty to forty families living in pyramid-shaped housing structures and was supported by a freshwater spring from the hillsides.[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)

### Spanish period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spanish period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/The_landing_of_Cabrillo_on_California_%28detail_from_mural_by_Daniel_Sayre_Groesbeck_at_the_Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse%29.tif/lossy-page1-250px-The_landing_of_Cabrillo_on_California_%28detail_from_mural_by_Daniel_Sayre_Groesbeck_at_the_Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse%29.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_landing_of_Cabrillo_on_California_(detail_from_mural_by_Daniel_Sayre_Groesbeck_at_the_Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse).tif)

Portuguese explorer [Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo](/wiki/Juan_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Cabrillo "Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo") landing in [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay") in 1542, claiming California for the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire")

The first European to visit the region was explorer [Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo](/wiki/Juan_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Cabrillo "Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo"). Sailing his flagship *San Salvador* from [Navidad](/wiki/Barra_de_Navidad "Barra de Navidad"), New Spain, Cabrillo claimed the bay for the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") in 1542, and named the site "San Miguel".[[29]](#cite_note-29) In November 1602, [Sebastián Vizcaíno](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Vizca%C3%ADno "Sebastián Vizcaíno") surveyed the harbor and what are now [Mission Bay](/wiki/Mission_Bay,_San_Diego,_California "Mission Bay, San Diego, California") and [Point Loma](/wiki/Point_Loma "Point Loma") and named the area for the Catholic [Saint Didacus](/wiki/Didacus_of_Alcal%C3%A1 "Didacus of Alcalá"), a [Spaniard](/wiki/Spaniard "Spaniard") more commonly known as *San Diego de Alcalá*.[[21]](#cite_note-:4-21)

The permanent [European colonization](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas") of both California and San Diego began in 1769 with the arrival of four contingents of Spaniards from New Spain and the [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") peninsula. Two seaborne parties reached San Diego Bay: the *San Carlos*, under Vicente Vila and including as notable members the engineer and cartographer [Miguel Costansó](/wiki/Miguel_Costans%C3%B3 "Miguel Costansó") and the soldier and future governor [Pedro Fages](/wiki/Pedro_Fages "Pedro Fages"), and the *San Antonio*, under [Juan Pérez](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_P%C3%A9rez_Hern%C3%A1ndez "Juan José Pérez Hernández"). An initial overland expedition to San Diego from the south was led by the soldier [Fernando Rivera](/wiki/Fernando_Rivera_y_Moncada "Fernando Rivera y Moncada") and included the [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") missionary, explorer, and chronicler [Juan Crespí](/wiki/Juan_Cresp%C3%AD "Juan Crespí"), followed by a second party led by the designated governor [Gaspar de Portolá](/wiki/Gaspar_de_Portol%C3%A1 "Gaspar de Portolá") and including the mission president [Junípero Serra](/wiki/Jun%C3%ADpero_Serra "Junípero Serra").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Mission_San_Diego%2C_c._1820.jpg/250px-Mission_San_Diego%2C_c._1820.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mission_San_Diego,_c._1820.jpg)

[Mission San Diego de Alcalá](/wiki/Mission_San_Diego_de_Alcal%C3%A1 "Mission San Diego de Alcalá") was founded in 1769 by [Saint Junípero Serra](/wiki/Saint_Jun%C3%ADpero_Serra "Saint Junípero Serra"), making it the oldest of the [Spanish missions in California](/wiki/Spanish_missions_in_California "Spanish missions in California").

In May 1769, Portolà established the [Presidio of San Diego](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Diego "Presidio of San Diego") on a hill near the [San Diego River](/wiki/San_Diego_River "San Diego River") above the Kumeyaay village of Cosoy,[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22) which would later become incorporated into the Spanish settlement,[[28]](#cite_note-:2-28) making it the first settlement by Europeans in what is now the state of California. In July of the same year, [Mission San Diego de Alcalá](/wiki/Mission_San_Diego_de_Alcal%C3%A1 "Mission San Diego de Alcalá") was founded by Franciscan friars under Serra.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The mission became a site for a Kumeyaay revolt in 1775, which forced the mission to relocate six miles (10 km) up the San Diego River.[[33]](#cite_note-33) By 1797, the mission boasted the largest native population in Alta California, with over 1,400 neophytes living in and around the mission proper.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Mission San Diego was the southern anchor in Alta California of the historic mission trail [El Camino Real](/wiki/El_Camino_Real_(California) "El Camino Real (California)"). Both the Presidio and the Mission are [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_San_Diego_County,_California "National Register of Historic Places listings in San Diego County, California").[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Mexican period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Mexican period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Estudillo.jpg/250px-Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Estudillo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Estudillo.jpg)

[José María Estudillo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Estudillo "José María Estudillo") served as commandant of the [Presidio of San Diego](/wiki/Presidio_of_San_Diego "Presidio of San Diego") and founded the [Estudillo family](/wiki/Estudillo_family_of_California "Estudillo family of California"), a powerful clan of [Californios](/wiki/Californio "Californio").

In 1821, Mexico [won its independence from Spain](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence"), and San Diego became part of the Mexican territory of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California"). In 1822, Mexico began its attempt to extend its authority over the coastal territory of Alta California. The fort on Presidio Hill was gradually abandoned, while the town of San Diego grew up on the level land below Presidio Hill. The Mission was [secularized by the Mexican government in 1834](/wiki/Mexican_secularization_act_of_1833 "Mexican secularization act of 1833"), and most of the Mission lands were granted to former soldiers. The 432 [residents](/wiki/Vecino "Vecino") of the town petitioned the governor to form a [pueblo](/wiki/Cabildo_(council) "Cabildo (council)"), and [Juan María Osuna](/wiki/Juan_Mar%C3%ADa_Osuna "Juan María Osuna") was elected the first *[alcalde](/wiki/Alcalde "Alcalde")* ("municipal magistrate"). Beyond the town, Mexican [land grants](/wiki/Land_grant "Land grant") expanded the number of [California ranchos](/wiki/Ranchos_of_California "Ranchos of California") that modestly added to the local economy.

However, San Diego had been losing population throughout the 1830s, this was due to increasing tension between the settlers and the indigenous [Kumeyaay](/wiki/Kumeyaay "Kumeyaay") and in 1838 the town lost its pueblo status because its size dropped to an estimated 100 to 150 residents.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The [ranchos](/wiki/Ranchos_of_California "Ranchos of California") in the San Diego region faced Kumeyaay raids in the late 1830s and the town itself faced raids in the 1840s.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Americans gained an increased awareness of California, and its commercial possibilities, from the writings of two countrymen involved in the often officially forbidden, to foreigners, but economically significant hide and tallow trade, where San Diego was a major port and the only one with an adequate harbor: [William Shaler](/wiki/William_Shaler "William Shaler")'s "Journal of a Voyage Between China and the North-Western Coast of America, Made in 1804" and [Richard Henry Dana](/wiki/Richard_Henry_Dana_Jr. "Richard Henry Dana Jr.")'s more substantial and convincing account, of his 1834–36 voyage, *[Two Years Before the Mast](/wiki/Two_Years_Before_the_Mast "Two Years Before the Mast")*.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Casa_de_Estudillo_courtyard_04.jpg/250px-Casa_de_Estudillo_courtyard_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_de_Estudillo_courtyard_04.jpg)

[Casa de Estudillo](/wiki/Casa_de_Estudillo "Casa de Estudillo"), built 1827, is one of San Diego's oldest buildings and served as inspiration for [Helen Hunt Jackson](/wiki/Helen_Hunt_Jackson "Helen Hunt Jackson")'s 1884 novel *[Ramona](/wiki/Ramona "Ramona")*.

In 1846, the United States went to war against Mexico and sent a naval and land [expedition to conquer Alta California](/wiki/Conquest_of_California "Conquest of California"). At first, they had an easy time of it, capturing the major ports including San Diego, but the Californios in southern Alta California struck back. Following the successful revolt in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), the American garrison at San Diego was driven out without firing a shot in early October 1846. Mexican partisans held San Diego for three weeks until October 24, 1846, when the Americans recaptured it. For the next several months the Americans were blockaded inside the pueblo. Skirmishes occurred daily and snipers shot into the town every night. The Californios drove cattle away from the pueblo hoping to starve the Americans and their Californio supporters out. On December 1, the American garrison learned that the dragoons of General [Stephen W. Kearney](/wiki/Stephen_W._Kearney "Stephen W. Kearney") were at [Warner's Ranch](/wiki/Warner%27s_Ranch "Warner's Ranch"). Commodore [Robert F. Stockton](/wiki/Robert_F._Stockton "Robert F. Stockton") sent a mounted force of fifty under Captain [Archibald Gillespie](/wiki/Archibald_Gillespie "Archibald Gillespie") to march north to meet him. Their joint command of 150 men, returning to San Diego, encountered about 93 Californios under [Andrés Pico](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Pico "Andrés Pico").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Battle_of_San_Pasqual_by_William_H_Meyers_c1846.jpg/250px-Battle_of_San_Pasqual_by_William_H_Meyers_c1846.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_San_Pasqual_by_William_H_Meyers_c1846.jpg)

The 1846 [Battle of San Pasqual](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Pasqual "Battle of San Pasqual") was a decisive battle between American and [Californio](/wiki/Californio "Californio") forces.

In the ensuing [Battle of San Pasqual](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Pasqual "Battle of San Pasqual"), fought in the [San Pasqual Valley](/wiki/San_Pasqual_Valley "San Pasqual Valley") which is now part of the city of San Diego, the Americans suffered their worst losses in the campaign. Subsequently, a column led by Lieutenant Gray arrived from San Diego, rescuing Kearny's command.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Stockton and Kearny went on to recover Los Angeles and force the capitulation of Alta California with the "[Treaty of Cahuenga](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cahuenga "Treaty of Cahuenga")" on January 13, 1847. As a result of the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") of 1846–48, the territory of Alta California, including San Diego, was ceded to the United States by Mexico, under the terms of the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") in 1848. The Mexican negotiators of that treaty tried to retain San Diego as part of Mexico, but the Americans insisted that San Diego was "for every commercial purpose of nearly equal importance to us with that of San Francisco", and the Mexican–American border was eventually established to be one league south of the southernmost point of [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### American period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: American period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/San_Diego_California_Looking_East_Across_the_Bay_by_Alfred_Mathews_1873.jpg/250px-San_Diego_California_Looking_East_Across_the_Bay_by_Alfred_Mathews_1873.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego_California_Looking_East_Across_the_Bay_by_Alfred_Mathews_1873.jpg)

View of [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay") in 1873 following the [U.S. conquest of California](/wiki/Conquest_of_California "Conquest of California")

The state of California was admitted to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in 1850. That same year San Diego was designated the seat of the newly established County of San Diego and was incorporated as a city. [Joshua H. Bean](/wiki/Joshua_H._Bean "Joshua H. Bean"), the last alcalde of San Diego, was elected the first mayor. Two years later the city was bankrupt;[[42]](#cite_note-42) the California legislature revoked the city's charter and placed it under control of a board of trustees, where it remained until 1889. A city charter was reestablished in 1889, and today's city charter was adopted in 1931.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The original town of San Diego was located at the foot of Presidio Hill, in the area which is now [Old Town San Diego State Historic Park](/wiki/Old_Town_San_Diego_State_Historic_Park "Old Town San Diego State Historic Park"). The location was not ideal, being several miles away from navigable water at its port at [La Playa](/wiki/La_Playa,_San_Diego "La Playa, San Diego"). In 1850, [William Heath Davis](/wiki/William_Heath_Davis "William Heath Davis") promoted a new development by the bay shore called "New San Diego", several miles south of the original settlement; however, for several decades the new development consisted only of a pier, a few houses and an [Army depot](/wiki/San_Diego_Barracks "San Diego Barracks") for the support of [Fort Yuma](/wiki/Fort_Yuma "Fort Yuma"). After 1854, the fort became supplied by sea and by [steamboats on the Colorado River](/wiki/Steamboats_of_the_Colorado_River "Steamboats of the Colorado River") and the depot fell into disuse. From 1857 to 1860, San Diego became the western terminus of the [San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line](/wiki/San_Antonio-San_Diego_Mail_Line "San Antonio-San Diego Mail Line"), the earliest overland [stagecoach](/wiki/Stagecoach "Stagecoach") and mail operation from the [Eastern United States](/wiki/Eastern_United_States "Eastern United States") to California, coming from [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") through [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory") in less than 30 days.[[44]](#cite_note-Pierce-44)

[![Oval, black and white shoulder-height portrait of a man in his forties or fifties, slightly balding wearing a suit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/View_of_the_US_Grant_with_the_main_fountain_at_the_entrance_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-View_of_the_US_Grant_with_the_main_fountain_at_the_entrance_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_the_US_Grant_with_the_main_fountain_at_the_entrance_(cropped).jpg)

[Horton Plaza](/wiki/Horton_Plaza_and_Broadway_Fountain "Horton Plaza and Broadway Fountain") honors [Alonzo Horton](/wiki/Alonzo_Horton "Alonzo Horton"), who helped develop [Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_San_Diego "Downtown San Diego").

In the late 1860s, [Alonzo Horton](/wiki/Alonzo_Horton "Alonzo Horton") promoted a move to the bayside area, which he called "New Town" and which became [downtown San Diego](/wiki/Downtown_San_Diego "Downtown San Diego"). Horton promoted the area heavily, and people and businesses began to relocate to New Town because its location on [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay") was convenient to shipping. New Town soon eclipsed the original settlement, known to this day as [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town,_San_Diego,_California "Old Town, San Diego, California"), and became the economic and governmental heart of the city.[[45]](#cite_note-Cornerstone-45) Still, San Diego remained a relative backwater town until the arrival of a railroad connection in 1878.

In 1912, San Diego was the site of a [free speech fight](/wiki/San_Diego_free_speech_fight "San Diego free speech fight") between the [Industrial Workers of the World](/wiki/Industrial_Workers_of_the_World "Industrial Workers of the World") and the city government who passed an ordinance forbidding the [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") along an area of "Soapbox Row" that led to civil disobedience, [vigilantism](/wiki/Vigilantism "Vigilantism"), [police violence](/wiki/Police_violence "Police violence"), the abduction of [Emma Goldman](/wiki/Emma_Goldman "Emma Goldman")'s husband [Ben Reitman](/wiki/Ben_Reitman "Ben Reitman") and [multiple riots](/wiki/San_Diego_free_speech_fight "San Diego free speech fight").[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) San Diego's proximity to Tijuana during the [Mexican Revolution](/wiki/Mexican_Revolution "Mexican Revolution") made this one of the most significant [free speech fights](/wiki/Free_speech_fights "Free speech fights") during the [Wobbly](/wiki/Wobbly "Wobbly") era.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In 1916, the neighborhood of [Stingaree](/wiki/Stingaree,_San_Diego "Stingaree, San Diego"), the original home of San Diego's first [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown "Chinatown") and "Soapbox Row", was demolished by anti-[vice](/wiki/Vice "Vice") campaigners to make way for the [Gaslamp Quarter](/wiki/Gaslamp_Quarter,_San_Diego "Gaslamp Quarter, San Diego").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![Hand drawn illustration of Balboa Park](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Guide_Book_of_the_Panama_California_Exposition_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Guide_Book_of_the_Panama_California_Exposition_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guide_Book_of_the_Panama_California_Exposition_(cropped).jpg)

[Balboa Park](/wiki/Balboa_Park_(San_Diego) "Balboa Park (San Diego)") was built for the [Panama-California Exposition of 1915](/wiki/Panama%E2%80%93California_Exposition "Panama–California Exposition").

In the early part of the 20th century, San Diego hosted the [World's Fair](/wiki/World%27s_Fair "World's Fair") twice: the [Panama–California Exposition](/wiki/Panama%E2%80%93California_Exposition "Panama–California Exposition") in 1915 and the [California Pacific International Exposition](/wiki/California_Pacific_International_Exposition "California Pacific International Exposition") in 1935. Both expositions were held in [Balboa Park](/wiki/Balboa_Park_(San_Diego) "Balboa Park (San Diego)"), and many of the Spanish/Baroque-style buildings that were built for those expositions remain to this day as central features of the park.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The menagerie of exotic animals featured at the 1915 exposition provided the basis for the [San Diego Zoo](/wiki/San_Diego_Zoo "San Diego Zoo").[[51]](#cite_note-51) During the 1950s there was a citywide festival called [Fiesta del Pacifico](/wiki/Fiesta_del_Pacifico "Fiesta del Pacifico") highlighting the area's Spanish and Mexican past.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

The southern portion of the [Point Loma](/wiki/Point_Loma,_San_Diego "Point Loma, San Diego") peninsula was set aside for military purposes as early as 1852. Over the next several decades the [Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") set up a series of coastal artillery batteries and named the area [Fort Rosecrans](/wiki/Fort_Rosecrans "Fort Rosecrans").[[53]](#cite_note-53) Significant U.S. Navy presence began in 1901 with the establishment of the Navy Coaling Station in Point Loma, and expanded greatly during the 1920s.[[54]](#cite_note-54) By 1930, the city was host to [Naval Base San Diego](/wiki/Naval_Base_San_Diego "Naval Base San Diego"), [Naval Training Center San Diego](/wiki/Naval_Training_Center_San_Diego "Naval Training Center San Diego"), [San Diego Naval Hospital](/wiki/San_Diego_Naval_Hospital "San Diego Naval Hospital"), [Camp Matthews](/wiki/Camp_Matthews "Camp Matthews"), and [Camp Kearny](/wiki/Camp_Kearny "Camp Kearny") (now [Marine Corps Air Station Miramar](/wiki/Marine_Corps_Air_Station_Miramar "Marine Corps Air Station Miramar")). The city was also an early center for aviation: as early as World War I, San Diego was proclaiming itself "The Air Capital of the West".[[55]](#cite_note-Shepherd-55) The city was home to important airplane developers and manufacturers like Ryan Airlines (later [Ryan Aeronautical](/wiki/Ryan_Aeronautical "Ryan Aeronautical")), founded in 1925, and [Consolidated Aircraft](/wiki/Consolidated_Aircraft "Consolidated Aircraft") (later [Convair](/wiki/Convair "Convair")), founded in 1923.[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Charles A. Lindbergh](/wiki/Charles_A._Lindbergh "Charles A. Lindbergh")'s plane, the *[Spirit of St. Louis](/wiki/Spirit_of_St._Louis "Spirit of St. Louis")*, was built in San Diego in 1927 by Ryan Airlines.[[55]](#cite_note-Shepherd-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Corner_of_San_Diego%27s_Fifth_Street_and_F_Street%2C_looking_north%2C_ca.1903_%28CHS-9776%29.jpg/250px-Corner_of_San_Diego%27s_Fifth_Street_and_F_Street%2C_looking_north%2C_ca.1903_%28CHS-9776%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corner_of_San_Diego%27s_Fifth_Street_and_F_Street,_looking_north,_ca.1903_(CHS-9776).jpg)

[Downtown San Diego](/wiki/Downtown_San_Diego "Downtown San Diego"), c. 1903

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), San Diego became a major hub of military and defense activity, due to the presence of so many military installations and defense manufacturers. The city's population grew rapidly during and after World War II, more than doubling between 1930 (147,995) and 1950 (333,865).[[57]](#cite_note-RM_54-57) During the final months of the war, the Japanese had a plan to target multiple U.S. cities for [biological attack](/wiki/Biological_attack "Biological attack"), starting with San Diego. The plan was called "[Operation Cherry Blossoms at Night](/wiki/Operation_Cherry_Blossoms_at_Night "Operation Cherry Blossoms at Night")" and called for [kamikaze](/wiki/Kamikaze "Kamikaze") planes filled with fleas infected with plague (*[Yersinia pestis](/wiki/Yersinia_pestis "Yersinia pestis")*) to crash into civilian population centers in the city, hoping to spread plague in the city and effectively kill tens of thousands of civilians. The plan was scheduled to launch on September 22, 1945, but the plan was not carried out because [Japan surrendered](/wiki/Japanese_surrender "Japanese surrender") five weeks earlier.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

After World War II, the military continued to play a major role in the local economy, but post–[Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") cutbacks took a heavy toll on the local defense and aerospace industries. The resulting downturn led San Diego leaders to seek to diversify the city's economy by focusing on research and science, as well as tourism.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

In the early 1960s, [Tom Hom](/wiki/Tom_Hom "Tom Hom") would become the first Asian American member of the San Diego City Council.[[62]](#cite_note-62) He would be succeeded by [Leon Williams](/wiki/Leon_Williams_(politician) "Leon Williams (politician)"), the first Black member of the city council.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Sdmarina.JPG/250px-Sdmarina.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sdmarina.JPG)

Starting in the 1980s, many areas of Downtown, such as the [Marina District](/wiki/Marina,_San_Diego "Marina, San Diego"), underwent [redevelopment](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal").

From the start of the 20th century through the 1970s, the American [tuna](/wiki/Tuna "Tuna") fishing fleet and tuna canning industry were based in San Diego, "the tuna capital of the world".[[64]](#cite_note-gala-64) San Diego's first tuna cannery was founded in 1911, and by the mid-1930s the canneries employed more than 1,000 people. A large fishing fleet supported the canneries, mostly staffed by immigrant fishermen from [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and later from the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") whose influence is still felt in neighborhoods like [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_San_Diego "Little Italy, San Diego") and [Point Loma](/wiki/Point_Loma,_San_Diego "Point Loma, San Diego").[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) Due to rising costs and foreign competition, the last of the canneries closed in the early 1980s.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Downtown San Diego was in decline in the 1960s and 1970s, but experienced some urban renewal since the early 1980s, including the opening of [Horton Plaza](/wiki/Westfield_Horton_Plaza "Westfield Horton Plaza"), the revival of the Gaslamp Quarter, and the construction of the [San Diego Convention Center](/wiki/San_Diego_Convention_Center "San Diego Convention Center"); [Petco Park](/wiki/Petco_Park "Petco Park") opened in 2004.[[68]](#cite_note-Erie-68) Outside of downtown, San Diego annexed large swaths of land and for suburban expansion to the north and control of the [San Ysidro Port of Entry](/wiki/San_Ysidro_Port_of_Entry "San Ysidro Port of Entry").

As the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") ended, the military shrank and so did defense spending. San Diego has since become a center of the emerging biotech industry and is home to telecommunications giant [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm"). San Diego had also grown in the tourism industry with the popularity of attractions such as the [San Diego Zoo](/wiki/San_Diego_Zoo "San Diego Zoo"), [SeaWorld San Diego](/wiki/SeaWorld_San_Diego "SeaWorld San Diego"), and [Legoland California](/wiki/Legoland_California "Legoland California") in [Carlsbad](/wiki/Carlsbad,_California "Carlsbad, California").[[69]](#cite_note-69)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

See also: [List of beaches in San Diego, California](/wiki/List_of_beaches_in_San_Diego,_California "List of beaches in San Diego, California") and [Parks in San Diego](/wiki/Parks_in_San_Diego "Parks in San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/San_Diego_with_Tijuana_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-03-09.jpg/250px-San_Diego_with_Tijuana_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-03-09.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego_with_Tijuana_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-03-09.jpg)

Satellite view of the [San Diego–Tijuana](/wiki/San_Diego%E2%80%93Tijuana "San Diego–Tijuana") area, a [transborder agglomeration](/wiki/Transborder_agglomeration "Transborder agglomeration") straddling the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border") in [the Californias](/wiki/The_Californias "The Californias")

According to SDSU professor emeritus Monte Marshall, [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay") is "the surface expression of a north-south-trending, nested [graben](/wiki/Graben "Graben")". The [Rose Canyon](/wiki/Rose_Canyon_Fault "Rose Canyon Fault") and [Point Loma](/wiki/Point_Loma_Formation "Point Loma Formation") [fault zones](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)") are part of the [San Andreas Fault](/wiki/San_Andreas_Fault "San Andreas Fault") system. About 40 miles (64 km) east of the bay are the [Laguna Mountains](/wiki/Laguna_Mountains "Laguna Mountains") in the [Peninsular Ranges](/wiki/Peninsular_Ranges "Peninsular Ranges"), which are part of the [American Cordillera](/wiki/American_Cordillera "American Cordillera").[[70]](#cite_note-Marshall-70)

The city lies on approximately 200 deep canyons and hills separating its [mesas](/wiki/Mesa "Mesa"), creating small pockets of natural open space scattered throughout the city and giving it a hilly geography.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Traditionally, San Diegans have built their homes and businesses on the mesas, while leaving the urban canyons relatively wild.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Thus, the canyons give parts of the city a segmented feel, creating gaps between otherwise proximate neighborhoods and contributing to a low-density, car-centered environment. The [San Diego River](/wiki/San_Diego_River "San Diego River") runs through the middle of San Diego from east to west, creating a river valley that serves to divide the city into northern and southern segments. Several reservoirs and [Mission Trails Regional Park](/wiki/Mission_Trails_Regional_Park "Mission Trails Regional Park") also lie between and separate developed areas of the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Torrey_Pines_cliffs.jpg/250px-Torrey_Pines_cliffs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torrey_Pines_cliffs.jpg)

[Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve](/wiki/Torrey_Pines_State_Natural_Reserve "Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve")

Notable peaks within the city limits include [Cowles Mountain](/wiki/Cowles_Mountain "Cowles Mountain"), the highest point in the city at 1,591 feet (485 m);[[9]](#cite_note-city_data-9) [Black Mountain](/wiki/Black_Mountain_Open_Space_Park "Black Mountain Open Space Park") at 1,558 feet (475 m); and [Mount Soledad](/wiki/Mount_Soledad "Mount Soledad") at 824 feet (251 m). The [Cuyamaca Mountains](/wiki/Cuyamaca_Mountains "Cuyamaca Mountains") and Laguna Mountains rise to the east of the city, and beyond the mountains are desert areas. [Cleveland National Forest](/wiki/Cleveland_National_Forest "Cleveland National Forest") is a half-hour drive from downtown San Diego. Numerous farms are found in the valleys northeast and southeast of the city.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main article: [Climate of San Diego](/wiki/Climate_of_San_Diego "Climate of San Diego")

Under the [Köppen–Geiger climate classification system](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen%E2%80%93Geiger_climate_classification_system "Köppen–Geiger climate classification system"), the San Diego area has been variously categorized as having either a [hot semi-arid climate](/wiki/Hot_semi-arid_climate "Hot semi-arid climate") (*[BSh](/wiki/Hot_semi-arid_climate "Hot semi-arid climate")* in the original classification[[74]](#cite_note-74) and *BSkn* in modified Köppen classification with the n denoting summer fog)[[75]](#cite_note-75) or a [hot-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Hot-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer Mediterranean climate")[[76]](#cite_note-76) (*Csa*).[[77]](#cite_note-77) San Diego's climate is characterized by warm, dry summers and mild winters, with most of the annual precipitation falling between December and March. The city has a mild climate year-round,[[78]](#cite_note-78) with an average of 201 days above 70 °F (21 °C) and low rainfall (9–13 inches [230–330 mm] annually).

The climate in San Diego, like most of Southern California, often varies significantly over short geographical distances, resulting in [microclimates](/wiki/Microclimate "Microclimate"). In San Diego, this is mostly because of the city's topography (the Bay, and the numerous hills, mountains, and canyons). Frequently, particularly during the "May gray/[June gloom](/wiki/June_Gloom "June Gloom")" period, a thick "[marine layer](/wiki/Marine_layer "Marine layer")" cloud cover keeps the air cool and damp within a few miles of the coast, but yields to bright cloudless sunshine approximately 5–10 miles (8–16 km) inland.[[79]](#cite_note-79) Sometimes the June gloom lasts into July, causing cloudy skies over most of San Diego for the entire day.[[80]](#cite_note-weather1-80)[[81]](#cite_note-weather_el_cajon-81) Even in the absence of June gloom, inland areas experience much more significant temperature variations than coastal areas, where the ocean serves as a moderating influence. Thus, for example, downtown San Diego averages January lows of 50 °F (10 °C) and August highs of 78 °F (26 °C). The city of [El Cajon](/wiki/El_Cajon,_California "El Cajon, California"), just 12 miles (19 km) inland from downtown San Diego, averages January lows of 42 °F (6 °C) and August highs of 88 °F (31 °C).

The average surface temperature of the water at Scripps Pier in the [California Current](/wiki/California_Current "California Current") has increased by almost 3 °F (1.7 °C) since 1950, according to scientists at [Scripps Institution of Oceanography](/wiki/Scripps_Institution_of_Oceanography "Scripps Institution of Oceanography").[[82]](#cite_note-82) Additionally, the mean minimum is now above 40 °F (4 °C), putting San Diego in [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 11, with the last freeze having occurred many decades ago.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/SurfPacificBeach.jpg/250px-SurfPacificBeach.jpg)](/wiki/File:SurfPacificBeach.jpg)

Surfers at [Pacific Beach](/wiki/Pacific_Beach,_San_Diego "Pacific Beach, San Diego")

Annual rainfall along the coast averages 10.65 inches (271 mm) and the median is 9.6 inches (240 mm).[[83]](#cite_note-83) The months of December through March supply most of the rain, with February the only month averaging 2 inches (51 mm) or more. The months of May through September tend to be almost completely dry. Although there are few wet days per month during the rainy period, rainfall can be heavy when it does fall. Rainfall is usually greater in the higher elevations of San Diego; some of the higher areas can receive 11–15 inches (280–380 mm) per year. Variability from year to year can be dramatic: in the wettest years of 1883/1884 and 1940/1941, more than 24 inches (610 mm) fell, whilst in the driest years there was as little as 3.2 inches (80 mm). The wettest month on record is December 1921 with 9.21 inches (234 mm).

Snow in the city is rare, having been observed only six times in the century and a half that records have been kept.[[84]](#cite_note-84) On February 21, 2019, snow fell and accumulated in residential areas of the city, but none fell in the downtown area.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ecology")]

See also: [California coastal sage and chaparral](/wiki/California_coastal_sage_and_chaparral "California coastal sage and chaparral")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Cabrillo_Monument_04.JPG/250px-Cabrillo_Monument_04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cabrillo_Monument_04.JPG)

View of [Coronado](/wiki/Coronado,_California "Coronado, California") from [Cabrillo National Monument](/wiki/Cabrillo_National_Monument "Cabrillo National Monument")

Like much of [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"), the majority of San Diego's current area was originally occupied on the west by [coastal sage scrub](/wiki/Coastal_sage_scrub "Coastal sage scrub") and on the east by [chaparral](/wiki/Chaparral "Chaparral"), plant communities made up mostly of drought-resistant shrubs.[[90]](#cite_note-92) The steep and varied topography and proximity to the ocean create a number of different habitats within the city limits, including [tidal marsh](/wiki/Tidal_marsh "Tidal marsh") and [canyons](/wiki/Canyons "Canyons"). The chaparral and coastal sage scrub habitats in low elevations along the coast are prone to [wildfire](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire"), and the rates of fire increased in the 20th century, due primarily to fires starting near the borders of urban and wild areas.[[91]](#cite_note-FireVegetation-93)

San Diego's broad city limits encompass a number of large nature preserves, including [Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve](/wiki/Torrey_Pines_State_Natural_Reserve "Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve"), [Los Peñasquitos Canyon Preserve](/wiki/Los_Pe%C3%B1asquitos_Canyon_Preserve "Los Peñasquitos Canyon Preserve"), and [Mission Trails Regional Park](/wiki/Mission_Trails_Regional_Park "Mission Trails Regional Park"). Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve and a coastal strip continuing to the north constitute one of only two locations where the rare species of Torrey Pine, *[Pinus torreyana](/wiki/Torrey_pine "Torrey pine")*, is found.[[92]](#cite_note-TorreyPine-94)
Due to the steep topography that prevents or discourages building, along with some efforts for preservation, there are also a large number of canyons within the city limits that serve as nature preserves, including [Switzer Canyon](/wiki/Switzer_Canyon "Switzer Canyon"), Tecolote Canyon Natural Park,[[93]](#cite_note-TecoloteCanyon-95) and Marian Bear Memorial Park in [San Clemente Canyon](/wiki/San_Clemente_Canyon "San Clemente Canyon"),[[94]](#cite_note-MarianBear-96) as well as a number of small parks and preserves.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Cowles_Mtn._from_Lake_Murray_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Cowles_Mtn._from_Lake_Murray_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cowles_Mtn._from_Lake_Murray_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Cowles Mountain](/wiki/Cowles_Mountain "Cowles Mountain") from [Lake Murray](/wiki/Lake_Murray_(California) "Lake Murray (California)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Old_Town%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%2811%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Old_Town%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%2811%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Town,_San_Diego,_CA,_USA_-_panoramio_(11)_(cropped).jpg)

Serra Museum at [Presidio Park](/wiki/Presidio_Park "Presidio Park")

San Diego County has one of the highest counts of animal and plant species that appear on the [endangered list](/wiki/United_States_Fish_and_Wildlife_Service_list_of_endangered_species "United States Fish and Wildlife Service list of endangered species") of counties in the United States.[[95]](#cite_note-legacy.utsandiego.com-97) Because of its diversity of habitat and its position on the [Pacific Flyway](/wiki/Pacific_Flyway "Pacific Flyway"), San Diego County has recorded 492 different bird species, more than any other region in the country.[[96]](#cite_note-98) San Diego always scores high in the number of bird species observed in the annual [Christmas Bird Count](/wiki/Christmas_Bird_Count "Christmas Bird Count"), sponsored by the [Audubon Society](/wiki/Audubon_Society "Audubon Society"), and it is known as one of the "birdiest" areas in the United States.[[97]](#cite_note-99)[[98]](#cite_note-100)

San Diego and its backcountry suffer from periodic wildfires. In October 2003, San Diego was the site of the [Cedar Fire](/wiki/Cedar_Fire_(2003) "Cedar Fire (2003)"), at that time the largest wildfire in California over the past century.[[99]](#cite_note-CedarLargeFire-101) The fire burned 280,000 acres (1,100 km2), killed 15 people, and destroyed more than 2,200 homes.[[100]](#cite_note-102) In addition to damage caused by the fire, smoke resulted in a significant increase in emergency room visits; the poor air quality caused San Diego County schools to close for a week.[[101]](#cite_note-103) The [October 2007 California wildfires](/wiki/October_2007_California_wildfires "October 2007 California wildfires") destroyed some areas, particularly within [Rancho Bernardo](/wiki/Rancho_Bernardo,_San_Diego "Rancho Bernardo, San Diego"), as well as the nearby communities of [Rancho Santa Fe](/wiki/Rancho_Santa_Fe,_California "Rancho Santa Fe, California") and [Ramona](/wiki/Ramona,_California "Ramona, California").[[95]](#cite_note-legacy.utsandiego.com-97)

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

Main article: [List of communities and neighborhoods of San Diego](/wiki/List_of_communities_and_neighborhoods_of_San_Diego "List of communities and neighborhoods of San Diego")

The City of San Diego recognizes 52 individual areas as Community Planning Areas.[[102]](#cite_note-104) Within a given planning area there may be several distinct neighborhoods. Altogether the city contains more than 100 identified [neighborhoods](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_San_Diego,_California "Neighborhoods of San Diego, California").

[Downtown San Diego](/wiki/Downtown_San_Diego "Downtown San Diego") is located on [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay"). [Balboa Park](/wiki/Balboa_Park_(San_Diego) "Balboa Park (San Diego)") encompasses several mesas and canyons to the northeast, surrounded by older, dense urban communities including [Hillcrest](/wiki/Hillcrest,_San_Diego "Hillcrest, San Diego") and [North Park](/wiki/North_Park,_San_Diego "North Park, San Diego"). To the east and southeast lie [City Heights](/wiki/City_Heights,_San_Diego "City Heights, San Diego"), the [College Area](/wiki/College_Area "College Area"), and [Southeast San Diego](/wiki/Southeast_San_Diego "Southeast San Diego"). To the north lies [Mission Valley](/wiki/Mission_Valley "Mission Valley") and [Interstate 8](/wiki/Interstate_8 "Interstate 8"). The communities north of the valley and freeway, and south of [Marine Corps Air Station Miramar](/wiki/Marine_Corps_Air_Station_Miramar "Marine Corps Air Station Miramar"), include [Clairemont](/wiki/Clairemont,_San_Diego "Clairemont, San Diego"), [Kearny Mesa](/wiki/Kearny_Mesa "Kearny Mesa"), [Tierrasanta](/wiki/Tierrasanta "Tierrasanta"), and [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo,_San_Diego "Navajo, San Diego"). Stretching north from Miramar are the northern suburbs of [Mira Mesa](/wiki/Mira_Mesa "Mira Mesa"), [Scripps Ranch](/wiki/Scripps_Ranch "Scripps Ranch"), [Rancho Peñasquitos](/wiki/Rancho_Pe%C3%B1asquitos "Rancho Peñasquitos"), and [Rancho Bernardo](/wiki/Rancho_Bernardo "Rancho Bernardo"). The far northeast portion of the city encompasses [Lake Hodges](/wiki/Lake_Hodges "Lake Hodges") and the [San Pasqual Valley](/wiki/San_Pasqual_Valley "San Pasqual Valley"), which holds an agricultural preserve. [Carmel Valley](/wiki/Carmel_Valley,_San_Diego "Carmel Valley, San Diego") and [Del Mar Heights](/wiki/Del_Mar_Heights,_San_Diego "Del Mar Heights, San Diego") occupy the northwest corner of the city. To their south are [Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve](/wiki/Torrey_Pines_State_Natural_Reserve "Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve") and the business center of the [Golden Triangle](/wiki/Golden_Triangle,_San_Diego "Golden Triangle, San Diego"). Further south are the beach and coastal communities of [La Jolla](/wiki/La_Jolla "La Jolla"), [Pacific Beach](/wiki/Pacific_Beach,_San_Diego "Pacific Beach, San Diego"), [Mission Beach](/wiki/Mission_Beach,_San_Diego "Mission Beach, San Diego"), and [Ocean Beach](/wiki/Ocean_Beach,_San_Diego "Ocean Beach, San Diego"). [Point Loma](/wiki/Point_Loma "Point Loma") occupies the peninsula across San Diego Bay from downtown. The communities of [South San Diego](/wiki/South_San_Diego "South San Diego") (an [Exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave")), such as [San Ysidro](/wiki/San_Ysidro,_San_Diego "San Ysidro, San Diego") and [Otay Mesa](/wiki/Otay_Mesa "Otay Mesa"), are located next to the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"), and are physically separated from the rest of the city by the cities of [National City](/wiki/National_City,_California "National City, California") and [Chula Vista](/wiki/Chula_Vista "Chula Vista"). A narrow strip of land at the bottom of San Diego Bay connects these southern neighborhoods with the rest of the city.[[103]](#cite_note-105)

For the most part, San Diego neighborhood boundaries tend to be understood by its residents based on geographical boundaries like canyons and street patterns.[[104]](#cite_note-106) The city recognized the importance of its neighborhoods when it organized its 2008 General Plan around the concept of a "City of Villages".[[105]](#cite_note-107)

### Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

Main article: [List of tallest buildings in San Diego](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_San_Diego "List of tallest buildings in San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/US_Navy_110604-N-NS602-574_Navy_and_Marine_Corps_personnel%2C_along_with_community_leaders_from_the_greater_San_Diego_area_come_together_to_commemora_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_110604-N-NS602-574_Navy_and_Marine_Corps_personnel,_along_with_community_leaders_from_the_greater_San_Diego_area_come_together_to_commemora_(cropped).jpg)

Aerial view of central San Diego

San Diego was originally centered on the [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town,_San_Diego "Old Town, San Diego") district, but by the late 1860s the focus had shifted to the bayfront, in the belief that this new location would increase trade. As the "New Town" – present-day Downtown – waterfront location quickly developed, it eclipsed Old Town as the center of San Diego.[[45]](#cite_note-Cornerstone-45)

The first skyscraper over 300 feet (91 m) in San Diego was the [El Cortez Hotel](/wiki/El_Cortez_(San_Diego) "El Cortez (San Diego)"), built in 1927; it was the tallest building in the city until 1963.[[106]](#cite_note-108) As time went on, multiple buildings claimed the title of San Diego's tallest skyscraper, including the [530 B Street](/wiki/530_B_Street "530 B Street") and [Symphony Towers](/wiki/Symphony_Towers "Symphony Towers"). Currently the tallest building in San Diego is [One America Plaza](/wiki/One_America_Plaza "One America Plaza"), standing 500 feet (150 m) tall, which was completed in 1991.[[107]](#cite_note-EmpOneAmer-109) The downtown skyline contains no [supertall](/wiki/Supertall "Supertall") buildings due to a regulation put in place by the [Federal Aviation Administration](/wiki/Federal_Aviation_Administration "Federal Aviation Administration") (FAA) in the 1970s, which set a 500 feet (152 m) limit on the height of buildings within a one-mile (1.6 km) radius of [San Diego International Airport](/wiki/San_Diego_International_Airport "San Diego International Airport").[[108]](#cite_note-500feetAirport-110) An iconic description of the skyline includes its skyscrapers being compared to the tools of a toolbox.[[109]](#cite_note-SkyGrowsUp-111)

There are [several new high-rises under construction](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_San_Diego "List of tallest buildings in San Diego"), including two that exceed 400 feet (122 m) in height.

### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2010")]

The city had a population of 1,307,402 according to the 2010 census, distributed over a land area of 372.1 square miles (963.7 km2).[[133]](#cite_note-136) The urban area of San Diego had a total population of 2,956,746, making it the [third-largest](/wiki/List_of_urbanized_areas_in_California_(by_population) "List of urbanized areas in California (by population)") in the state, after those of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles_metropolitan_area "Los Angeles metropolitan area") and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_metropolitan_area "San Francisco metropolitan area").

The 2010 population represented an increase of just under 7% from the 1,223,400 people reported in 2000.[[129]](#cite_note-Census_SD_quickfacts-131) The population density was 3,771.9 inhabitants per square mile (1,456.3/km2). The racial makeup of San Diego was 58.9% [White](/wiki/White_American "White American"), 6.7% Black, 0.6% Native American, 15.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American") (5.9% [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_American "Filipino American"), 2.7% [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_American "Chinese American"), 2.5% [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_American "Vietnamese American"), 1.3% [Indian](/wiki/Indian_American "Indian American"), 1.0% [Korean](/wiki/Korean_American "Korean American"), 0.7% [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_American "Japanese American"), 0.4% [Laotian](/wiki/Laotian_American "Laotian American"), 0.3% [Cambodian](/wiki/Cambodian_American "Cambodian American"), 0.1% [Thai](/wiki/Thai_American "Thai American")). 0.5% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_American "Pacific Islander American") (0.2% [Guamanian](/wiki/Guamanian "Guamanian"), 0.1% [Samoan](/wiki/Samoan_American "Samoan American"), 0.1% [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiian "Native Hawaiian")), 12.3% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 5.1% from two or more races. 28.8% of the population was [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") (of any race);[[129]](#cite_note-Census_SD_quickfacts-131)[[134]](#cite_note-census2010-137) 24.9% of the total population was of [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American") heritage, 1.4% [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_American "Spanish American") and 0.6% [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people"). The median age of Hispanic residents was 27.5 years, compared to 35.1 years overall and 41.6 years among non-Hispanic whites; Hispanic San Diegans were the largest group under the age of 18, while non-Hispanic whites constituted 63.1% of population 55 and older.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_San_Diego_%285560483270%29.png/250px-Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_San_Diego_%285560483270%29.png)](/wiki/File:Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_San_Diego_(5560483270).png)

Map of racial distribution in San Diego, 2010 U.S. Census. Each dot is 25 people: ⬤ Non-Hispanic White ⬤ Black ⬤ Asian ⬤ Hispanic ⬤ Other

As of January 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit), the San Diego City and County had the fifth-largest [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_United_States "Homelessness in the United States") population among major cities in the United States, with 8,102 people experiencing homelessness.[[135]](#cite_note-138) In the city of San Diego, 4,887 individuals were experiencing homelessness according to the 2020 count.[[136]](#cite_note-139) A December 11, 2023, article in *[The San Diego Union-Tribune](/wiki/The_San_Diego_Union-Tribune "The San Diego Union-Tribune")* by Blake Nelson reports a notable decline in the homeless population in downtown San Diego, specifically in the urban core. According to data from the Downtown San Diego Partnership, the number of individuals living outside or in vehicles has reached a two-year low, standing at approximately 1,200 as of last month. The decrease is attributed to the implementation of the city's camping ban and the concerted efforts to establish new shelters. While enforcement has led to relatively few individuals being punished, the threat of legal consequences appears to have played a role in the reduction.[[137]](#cite_note-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Barrio_Logan%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%288%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Barrio_Logan%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%288%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barrio_Logan,_San_Diego,_CA,_USA_-_panoramio_(8)_(cropped).jpg)

[Barrio Logan](/wiki/Barrio_Logan "Barrio Logan") is a [Chicano](/wiki/Chicano "Chicano") cultural hub and [ethnic enclave](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave").

In 2000 there were 451,126 households, out of which 30.2% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 44.6% were married couples living together, 11.4% had a female householder with no husband present, and 39.8% were non-families. Households made up of individuals account for 28.0%, and 7.4% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.61, and the average family size was 3.30.

The U.S. Census Bureau reported that in 2000, 24.0% of San Diego residents were under 18, and 10.5% were 65 and over.[[129]](#cite_note-Census_SD_quickfacts-131) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit) the median age was 35.6; more than a quarter of residents were under age 20 and 11% were over age 65.[[138]](#cite_note-141) [Millennials](/wiki/Millennials "Millennials") (ages 26 through 42) constitute 27.1% of San Diego's population, the second-highest percentage in a major U.S. city.[[139]](#cite_note-142) The San Diego County regional planning agency, SANDAG, provides tables and graphs breaking down the city population into five-year age groups.[[140]](#cite_note-143)

In 2000, the [median income for a household](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") in the city was $45,733, and the median income for a family was $53,060. Males had a median income of $36,984 versus $31,076 for females. The per capita income for the city was $35,199.[[141]](#cite_note-2000SDCensus-144) According to *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* in 2005, San Diego was the fifth wealthiest U.S. city,[[142]](#cite_note-ForbesRichestCities-145) but about 10.6% of families and 14.6% of the population were below the poverty line, including 20.0% of those under age 18 and 7.6% of those age 65 or over.[[141]](#cite_note-2000SDCensus-144) As of January 1, 2008, estimates by the [San Diego Association of Governments](/wiki/San_Diego_Association_of_Governments "San Diego Association of Governments") revealed that the household median income for San Diego rose to $66,715, up from $45,733 in 2000.[[143]](#cite_note-sandag-146)

San Diego was named the ninth-most [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States")-friendly city in the U.S. in 2013.[[144]](#cite_note-147) The city also has the [seventh-highest population](/wiki/Top_US_Gay_Populations "Top US Gay Populations") of gay residents in the U.S. Additionally in 2013, [San Diego State University](/wiki/San_Diego_State_University#LGBT-Friendly_campus "San Diego State University") (SDSU), one of the city's prominent universities, was named one of the top LGBT-friendly campuses in the nation.[[145]](#cite_note-148)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Hsi_Fang_Temple_interior_San_Diego.jpg/250px-Hsi_Fang_Temple_interior_San_Diego.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hsi_Fang_Temple_interior_San_Diego.jpg)

Hsi Fang Buddhist temple in [University Heights](/wiki/University_Heights,_San_Diego "University Heights, San Diego").

According to a 2023-24 study by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), 53 percent of the city identified themselves as [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"). Among Christians, 25% professed adherence to various [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") churches, 21% professed [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") beliefs, and 3% identified as [Latter-day Saint (Mormon)](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints")[[146]](#cite_note-149) In the same study, 40% of San Diegans claimed [no religious affiliation](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion"). The same study found that followers of other religions (including [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism")) collectively made up about 6% of the population.

### Foreign-born population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Foreign-born population")]

The majority of San Diego's foreign-born population were born in Mexico, the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), China and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam").[[147]](#cite_note-150)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of San Diego](/wiki/Economy_of_San_Diego "Economy of San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/FA18CHornetOverSanDiegoNov08.jpg/250px-FA18CHornetOverSanDiegoNov08.jpg)](/wiki/File:FA18CHornetOverSanDiegoNov08.jpg)

An [F/A-18 Hornet](/wiki/McDonnell_Douglas_F/A-18_Hornet "McDonnell Douglas F/A-18 Hornet") flying over San Diego. The city serves as a major hub for the [defense industry](/wiki/Defense_industry "Defense industry") and [U.S. military](/wiki/U.S._military "U.S. military").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/OneAmericaPlazaSDSept10.jpg/250px-OneAmericaPlazaSDSept10.jpg)](/wiki/File:OneAmericaPlazaSDSept10.jpg)

[One America Plaza](/wiki/One_America_Plaza "One America Plaza") is the [tallest building in San Diego](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_San_Diego "List of tallest buildings in San Diego").

The largest sectors of San Diego's economy are [defense/military](/wiki/Defense_industry "Defense industry"), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade"), and [research](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development")/[manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing").[[148]](#cite_note-151)[[149]](#cite_note-TribuneEconomySectors-152)
San Diego recorded a [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") of $79,646 in 2018, an increase of 3.89% from $76,662 in 2017.[[150]](#cite_note-datausa-153) The median property value in San Diego in 2018 was $654,700,[[150]](#cite_note-datausa-153) and the average home has two cars per household.[[150]](#cite_note-datausa-153)
