### Defense and military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Defense and military")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/An_MH-60R_Sea_Hawk_helicopter_flies_over_San_Diego._%2824546100368%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-An_MH-60R_Sea_Hawk_helicopter_flies_over_San_Diego._%2824546100368%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:An_MH-60R_Sea_Hawk_helicopter_flies_over_San_Diego._(24546100368)_(cropped).jpg)

View of [Naval Base San Diego](/wiki/Naval_Base_San_Diego "Naval Base San Diego")

The economy of San Diego is influenced by [its deepwater port](/wiki/Port_of_San_Diego "Port of San Diego"), which includes the only major submarine and shipbuilding yards on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States").[[152]](#cite_note-155) Several major national [defense contractors](/wiki/Defense_contractor "Defense contractor") were started and are headquartered in San Diego, including [General Atomics](/wiki/General_Atomics "General Atomics"), [Cubic](/wiki/Cubic_Corporation "Cubic Corporation"), and [NASSCO](/wiki/National_Steel_and_Shipbuilding_Company "National Steel and Shipbuilding Company").[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-157)

San Diego hosts the largest naval fleet in the world:[[155]](#cite_note-158) In 2008 it was home to 53 ships, over 120 tenant commands, and more than 35,000 sailors, marines, [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") civilian employees and contractors.[[156]](#cite_note-Navy.mil-159) About 5 percent of all civilian jobs in the county are military-related, and 15,000 businesses in San Diego County rely on Department of Defense contracts.[[156]](#cite_note-Navy.mil-159)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Defense.gov_photo_essay_100813-D-7203C-008_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Defense.gov_photo_essay_100813-D-7203C-008_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Defense.gov_photo_essay_100813-D-7203C-008_(cropped).jpg)

[Marine Corps Recruit Depot](/wiki/Marine_Corps_Recruit_Depot_San_Diego "Marine Corps Recruit Depot San Diego")

Military bases in San Diego include [US Navy](/wiki/US_Navy "US Navy") facilities, [Marine Corps](/wiki/USMC "USMC") bases, and [Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard") stations.
The city is "home to the majority of the U.S. Pacific Fleet's surface combatants, all of the Navy's West Coast amphibious ships and a variety of Coast Guard and Military Sealift Command vessels".[[156]](#cite_note-Navy.mil-159)[[157]](#cite_note-160)

The military infrastructure in San Diego is still growing and developing, with numerous military personnel stationed there, numbers of which are expected to rise. This plays a significant role in the city's economy, as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit), it provides roughly 25% of the GDP and provides 23% of the total jobs in San Diego.[[158]](#cite_note-161)[[159]](#cite_note-162)[[160]](#cite_note-163)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Balboa_Park%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%28224%29.jpg/250px-Balboa_Park%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%28224%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balboa_Park,_San_Diego,_CA,_USA_-_panoramio_(224).jpg)

[Casa de Balboa](/wiki/Casa_de_Balboa "Casa de Balboa") at [Balboa Park](/wiki/Balboa_Park_(San_Diego) "Balboa Park (San Diego)") is home to the [San Diego History Center](/wiki/San_Diego_History_Center "San Diego History Center").

Tourism is a major industry owing to the city's climate, [beaches](/wiki/Beaches_in_San_Diego,_California "Beaches in San Diego, California"),[[161]](#cite_note-164) and tourist attractions such as [Balboa Park](/wiki/Balboa_Park_(San_Diego) "Balboa Park (San Diego)"), [Belmont Park](/wiki/Belmont_Park_(San_Diego) "Belmont Park (San Diego)"), [San Diego Zoo](/wiki/San_Diego_Zoo "San Diego Zoo"), [San Diego Zoo Safari Park](/wiki/San_Diego_Zoo_Safari_Park "San Diego Zoo Safari Park"), and [SeaWorld San Diego](/wiki/SeaWorld_San_Diego "SeaWorld San Diego"). San Diego's Spanish and Mexican heritage is reflected in many historic sites across the city, such as [Mission San Diego de Alcalá](/wiki/Mission_San_Diego_de_Alcal%C3%A1 "Mission San Diego de Alcalá") and [Old Town San Diego State Historic Park](/wiki/Old_Town_San_Diego_State_Historic_Park "Old Town San Diego State Historic Park"). Also, the [local craft brewing industry](/wiki/Beer_in_San_Diego_County,_California "Beer in San Diego County, California") attracts an increasing number of visitors[[162]](#cite_note-165) for "beer tours" and the annual San Diego Beer Week in November;[[163]](#cite_note-166) San Diego has been called "America's Craft Beer Capital".[[164]](#cite_note-167)

San Diego County hosted more than 32 million visitors in 2012; collectively they spent an estimated $8 billion. The visitor industry provides employment for more than 160,000 people.[[165]](#cite_note-sandiego.org-168)

San Diego's cruise ship industry used to be the second-largest in California. Numerous cruise lines operate out of San Diego. However, cruise ship business has been in decline since 2008, when the Port hosted over 250 ship calls and more than 900,000 passengers. By 2016–2017, the number of ship calls had fallen to 90.[[166]](#cite_note-MexicanRivieraLikelyEnd-169)

Local sightseeing cruises are offered in San Diego Bay and Mission Bay, as well as whale-watching cruises to observe the migration of [gray whales](/wiki/Gray_whale "Gray whale"), peaking in mid-January.[[167]](#cite_note-InTheCitySanDiego.com-170) [Sport fishing](/wiki/Sport_fishing "Sport fishing") is another popular tourist attraction; San Diego is home to southern California's biggest sport fishing fleet.[[168]](#cite_note-171)

### International trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: International trade")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Cruise_Ships_Visit_Port_of_San_Diego_005_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Cruise_Ships_Visit_Port_of_San_Diego_005_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cruise_Ships_Visit_Port_of_San_Diego_005_(cropped).jpg)

The [Port of San Diego](/wiki/Port_of_San_Diego "Port of San Diego") is the third-busiest port in [California](/wiki/California "California").

San Diego's commercial port and its location on the [United States–Mexico border](/wiki/United_States%E2%80%93Mexico_border "United States–Mexico border") make international trade an important factor in the city's economy. The city is authorized by the United States government to operate as a [foreign-trade zone](/wiki/Foreign-trade_zone "Foreign-trade zone").[[169]](#cite_note-172)

The city shares a 15-mile (24 km) border with Mexico that includes two border crossings. San Diego hosts the busiest international border crossing in the world, in the San Ysidro neighborhood at the [San Ysidro Port of Entry](/wiki/San_Ysidro_Land_Port_of_Entry_Expansion_Project "San Ysidro Land Port of Entry Expansion Project").[[170]](#cite_note-173) A second, primarily commercial border crossing operates in the [Otay Mesa](/wiki/Otay_Mesa,_San_Diego "Otay Mesa, San Diego") area; it is the largest commercial crossing on the California–[Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") border and handles the third-highest volume of trucks and dollar value of trade among all United States-Mexico land crossings.[[171]](#cite_note-174)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/San_Ysidro_Border_Traffic_%288653120372%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Ysidro_Border_Traffic_%288653120372%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Ysidro_Border_Traffic_(8653120372)_(cropped).jpg)

[San Ysidro Port of Entry](/wiki/San_Ysidro_Port_of_Entry "San Ysidro Port of Entry") is the [4th-busiest border crossing in the world](/wiki/Border_checkpoint#Busiest_checkpoints_in_the_world "Border checkpoint") and the busiest in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").

The [Port of San Diego](/wiki/Port_of_San_Diego "Port of San Diego") is the third-busiest port in California and one of the busiest on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States"). One of the Port of San Diego's two [cargo](/wiki/Cargo "Cargo") facilities is located in [downtown San Diego](/wiki/Downtown_San_Diego "Downtown San Diego") at the Tenth Avenue Marine Terminal. This terminal has facilities for [containers](/wiki/Shipping_container "Shipping container"), [bulk cargo](/wiki/Bulk_cargo "Bulk cargo"), and [refrigerated](/wiki/Refrigerated "Refrigerated") and frozen storage, so that it can handle the import and export of many commodities.[[172]](#cite_note-175) In 2009 the Port of San Diego handled 1,137,054 short tons of total trade; foreign trade accounted for 956,637 short tons while domestic trade amounted to 180,417 short tons.[[173]](#cite_note-176)

Historically tuna fishing and canning was one of San Diego's major industries,[[174]](#cite_note-177) although the American tuna fishing fleet is no longer based in San Diego. Seafood company [Bumble Bee Foods](/wiki/Bumble_Bee_Foods "Bumble Bee Foods") is headquartered in San Diego, as was [Chicken of the Sea](/wiki/Chicken_of_the_Sea "Chicken of the Sea") until 2018.[[175]](#cite_note-178)[[176]](#cite_note-179)

### Companies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Companies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/AT%26TBuildingSanDiegoApr09.jpg/250px-AT%26TBuildingSanDiegoApr09.jpg)](/wiki/File:AT%26TBuildingSanDiegoApr09.jpg)

The [AT&T Building](/wiki/AT%26T_Building_(San_Diego) "AT&T Building (San Diego)")

San Diego hosts several major producers of wireless cellular technology. [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm") was founded and is headquartered in San Diego, and is one of the largest private-sector employers in San Diego.[[177]](#cite_note-SDLargeEmployers-180) Other wireless industry manufacturers with U.S. headquarters here include [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia"), [LG Electronics](/wiki/LG_Electronics "LG Electronics"),[[178]](#cite_note-181) [Kyocera International](/wiki/Kyocera_International "Kyocera International"),[[179]](#cite_note-182) [Cricket Communications](/wiki/Cricket_Communications "Cricket Communications") and Novatel Wireless.[[180]](#cite_note-183) San Diego also has the U.S. headquarters for the Slovak security company [ESET](/wiki/ESET "ESET").[[181]](#cite_note-184) San Diego has been designated as an iHub Innovation Center for potential collaboration between wireless and the life sciences.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

The [University of California, San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego") and other research institutions have helped to fuel the growth of [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology").[[183]](#cite_note-UCSDIsrael-186) In 2013, San Diego had the second-largest biotech cluster in the United States, below [Greater Boston](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston") and above the [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area").[[184]](#cite_note-187) There are more than 400 biotechnology companies in the area.[[185]](#cite_note-188) In particular, the [La Jolla](/wiki/La_Jolla,_San_Diego,_California "La Jolla, San Diego, California") and nearby [Sorrento Valley](/wiki/Sorrento_Valley,_San_Diego,_California "Sorrento Valley, San Diego, California") areas are home to offices and research facilities for numerous biotechnology companies.[[186]](#cite_note-Walcott-189) Major biotechnology companies like [Illumina](/wiki/Illumina_(company) "Illumina (company)") and [Neurocrine Biosciences](/wiki/Neurocrine_Biosciences "Neurocrine Biosciences") are headquartered in San Diego, while many other biotech and pharmaceutical companies have offices or research facilities in San Diego. San Diego is also home to more than 140 [contract research organizations](/wiki/Contract_research_organization "Contract research organization") (CROs) that provide contract services for pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies.[[187]](#cite_note-190)

### Real estate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Real estate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Village_of_La_Jolla%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA_92037%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%2848%29.jpg/250px-Village_of_La_Jolla%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA_92037%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%2848%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Village_of_La_Jolla,_San_Diego,_CA_92037,_USA_-_panoramio_(48).jpg)

[La Jolla](/wiki/La_Jolla "La Jolla") is a highly valued [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") market in San Diego.

San Diego has high real estate prices. San Diego home prices peaked in 2005, and then declined along with the national trend. As of December 2010, prices were down 36 percent from the peak,[[188]](#cite_note-191) [median price of homes](/wiki/Median_home_price "Median home price") having declined by more than $200,000 between 2005 and 2010.[[189]](#cite_note-192) As of May 2015, the median price of a house was $520,000.[[190]](#cite_note-193) In November 2018 the median home price was $558,000. The San Diego metropolitan area had one of the worst housing affordability rankings of all metropolitan areas in the United States in 2009.[[191]](#cite_note-194) The San Diego Housing Market experienced a decline in the median sold price of existing single-family homes between December 2022 and January 2023, with a 2.9% decrease from $850,000 to $824,950.[[192]](#cite_note-195) As of 2023, the majority of homes (nearly 60%) in San Diego are listed above $1 million, with the city's median home price at $910,000, ranking it fourth highest among the 30 largest U.S. cities.[[193]](#cite_note-196)[[194]](#cite_note-197)

Consequently, San Diego has experienced negative net migration since 2004. A significant number of people have moved to adjacent [Riverside County](/wiki/Riverside_County,_California "Riverside County, California"), commuting daily to jobs in San Diego, while others are leaving the area altogether and moving to more affordable regions.[[195]](#cite_note-SDGreenerPastures-198)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Government")]

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Local government")]

See also: [Mayor of San Diego](/wiki/Mayor_of_San_Diego "Mayor of San Diego"), [San Diego City Council](/wiki/San_Diego_City_Council "San Diego City Council"), and [Government of San Diego County](/wiki/Government_of_San_Diego_County,_California "Government of San Diego County, California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Cabrillo_Bridge_Reenactment_150509-M-PG109-006_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Cabrillo_Bridge_Reenactment_150509-M-PG109-006_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cabrillo_Bridge_Reenactment_150509-M-PG109-006_(cropped).jpg)

[Todd Gloria](/wiki/Todd_Gloria "Todd Gloria") is the current [mayor of San Diego](/wiki/Mayor_of_San_Diego "Mayor of San Diego").

The city is governed by a mayor and a nine-member city council. In 2006, its government changed from a [council–manager government](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government") to a [strong mayor government](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government"), as decided by a citywide vote in 2004. The mayor is in effect the chief executive officer of the city, while the council is the legislative body.[[196]](#cite_note-199) The City of San Diego is responsible for [police](/wiki/San_Diego_Police_Department "San Diego Police Department"), public safety, streets, water and sewer service, planning and zoning, and similar services within its borders. San Diego is a [sanctuary city](/wiki/Sanctuary_city "Sanctuary city"),[[197]](#cite_note-200) however, San Diego County is a participant of the [Secure Communities](/wiki/Secure_Communities_and_administrative_immigration_policies "Secure Communities and administrative immigration policies") program.[[198]](#cite_note-201)[[199]](#cite_note-202) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit), the city had one employee for every 137 residents, with a payroll greater than $733 million.[[200]](#cite_note-203)

The members of the city council are each elected from single-member districts within the city. The mayor and city attorney are elected directly by the voters of the entire city. The mayor, city attorney, and council members are elected to four-year terms, with a two-term limit.[[201]](#cite_note-204) Elections are held on a non-partisan basis per California state law; nevertheless, most officeholders do identify themselves as either Democrats or Republicans. In 2007, registered Democrats outnumbered Republicans by about 7 to 6 in the city,[[202]](#cite_note-RegDEMREP-205) and Democrats currently (as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit)) hold all nine seats on the city council. The current mayor, [Todd Gloria](/wiki/Todd_Gloria "Todd Gloria"), is a member of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/San_Diego_City_and_Administration_Building.jpg/250px-San_Diego_City_and_Administration_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego_City_and_Administration_Building.jpg)

[County Administration Center](/wiki/San_Diego_County_Administration_Center "San Diego County Administration Center"), seat of [San Diego County Government](/wiki/Government_of_San_Diego_County,_California "Government of San Diego County, California")

San Diego is part of [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County "San Diego County"), and includes all or part of the 1st, 2nd, 3rd and 4th supervisorial districts of the [San Diego County Board of Supervisors](/wiki/Government_of_San_Diego_County,_California#Board_of_Supervisors "Government of San Diego County, California"),[[203]](#cite_note-206) Other county officers elected in part by city residents include the [Sheriff](/wiki/San_Diego_County_Sheriff "San Diego County Sheriff"), [District Attorney](/wiki/San_Diego_County_District_Attorney "San Diego County District Attorney"), Assessor/Recorder/County Clerk, and Treasurer/Tax Collector.

Areas of the city immediately adjacent to [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay") ("[tidelands](/wiki/Tidelands "Tidelands")") are administered by the [Port of San Diego](/wiki/Port_of_San_Diego "Port of San Diego"), a quasi-governmental agency which owns all the property in the tidelands and is responsible for its land use planning, policing, and similar functions. San Diego is a member of the regional planning agency [San Diego Association of Governments](/wiki/San_Diego_Association_of_Governments "San Diego Association of Governments") (SANDAG). Public schools within the city are managed and funded by independent school districts (see [below](#Education)).

After narrowly supporting [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") in 1964, San Diego provided majorities to all six Republican presidential candidates from 1968 to 1988. However, in more recent decades, San Diego has trended in favor of [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") presidential candidates for president. [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in 1988 is the last Republican candidate to carry San Diego in a presidential election.

### State and federal representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: State and federal representation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/San_Diego%2C_2016_-_301.jpg/250px-San_Diego%2C_2016_-_301.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego,_2016_-_301.jpg)

San Diego Hall of Justice

In the [California State Senate](/wiki/California_State_Senate "California State Senate"), San Diego County encompasses the [38th](/wiki/California%27s_38th_State_Senate_district "California's 38th State Senate district"), [39th](/wiki/California%27s_39th_State_Senate_district "California's 39th State Senate district") and [40th](/wiki/California%27s_40th_State_Senate_district "California's 40th State Senate district") districts,[[204]](#cite_note-207) represented by [Catherine Blakespear](/wiki/Catherine_Blakespear "Catherine Blakespear") ([D](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party")), [Akilah Weber](/wiki/Akilah_Weber "Akilah Weber") ([D](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party")), and [Brian Jones](/wiki/Brian_Jones_(politician) "Brian Jones (politician)") ([R](/wiki/California_Republican_Party "California Republican Party")), respectively.

In the [California State Assembly](/wiki/California_State_Assembly "California State Assembly"), lying partially within the city of San Diego are the [77th](/wiki/California%27s_77th_State_Assembly_district "California's 77th State Assembly district"), [78th](/wiki/California%27s_78th_State_Assembly_district "California's 78th State Assembly district"), [79th](/wiki/California%27s_79th_State_Assembly_district "California's 79th State Assembly district"), and [80th](/wiki/California%27s_80th_State_Assembly_district "California's 80th State Assembly district") districts,[[205]](#cite_note-208) represented by [Tasha Boerner](/wiki/Tasha_Boerner "Tasha Boerner") ([D](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party")), [Chris Ward](/wiki/Chris_Ward_(California_politician) "Chris Ward (California politician)") ([D](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party")), [LaShae Sharp-Collins](/wiki/LaShae_Sharp-Collins "LaShae Sharp-Collins") ([D](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party")), and [David Alvarez](/wiki/David_Alvarez_(politician) "David Alvarez (politician)") ([D](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party")), respectively.

In the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), San Diego County includes parts or all of California's [48th](/wiki/California%27s_48th_congressional_district "California's 48th congressional district"), [49th](/wiki/California%27s_49th_congressional_district "California's 49th congressional district"), [50th](/wiki/California%27s_50th_congressional_district "California's 50th congressional district"), [51st](/wiki/California%27s_51st_congressional_district "California's 51st congressional district"), and [52nd](/wiki/California%27s_52nd_congressional_district "California's 52nd congressional district") congressional districts,[[206]](#cite_note-209) represented by [Darrell Issa](/wiki/Darrell_Issa "Darrell Issa") ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")), [Mike Levin](/wiki/Mike_Levin "Mike Levin") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")), [Scott Peters](/wiki/Scott_Peters_(politician) "Scott Peters (politician)") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")), [Sara Jacobs](/wiki/Sara_Jacobs "Sara Jacobs") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")), and [Juan Vargas](/wiki/Juan_Vargas "Juan Vargas") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")) respectively.

### Scandals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Scandals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Jacob_Weinberger_U.S._Courthouse%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA_Jun_03_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jacob_Weinberger_U.S._Courthouse%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA_Jun_03_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacob_Weinberger_U.S._Courthouse,_San_Diego,_CA_Jun_03_(cropped).jpg)

[Weinberger U.S. Courthouse](/wiki/Jacob_Weinberger_United_States_Courthouse "Jacob Weinberger United States Courthouse")

San Diego was the site of the 1912 [San Diego free speech fight](/wiki/San_Diego_free_speech_fight "San Diego free speech fight"), in which the city restricted speech, vigilantes brutalized and tortured anarchists, and the [San Diego Police Department](/wiki/San_Diego_Police_Department "San Diego Police Department") killed a member of the [Industrial Workers of the World](/wiki/Industrial_Workers_of_the_World "Industrial Workers of the World") (IWW).

In 1916, [rainmaker](/wiki/Rainmaking "Rainmaking") [Charles Hatfield](/wiki/Charles_Hatfield "Charles Hatfield") was blamed for $4 million in damages and accused of causing San Diego's worst [flood](/wiki/Floods_in_the_United_States:_1901%E2%80%932000#Southern_California_floods_-_January_1916 "Floods in the United States: 1901–2000"), during which about 20 [Japanese American](/wiki/Japanese_American "Japanese American") farmers died.[[207]](#cite_note-210)

Then-mayor [Roger Hedgecock](/wiki/Roger_Hedgecock "Roger Hedgecock") was forced to resign his post in 1985, after he was found guilty of one count of [conspiracy](/wiki/Conspiracy_(criminal) "Conspiracy (criminal)") and 12 counts of [perjury](/wiki/Perjury "Perjury"), related to the alleged failure to report all [campaign contributions](/wiki/Campaign_finance "Campaign finance").[[208]](#cite_note-Horstman-211)[[209]](#cite_note-Abrahamson-212) After a series of appeals, the 12 perjury counts were dismissed in 1990 based on claims of [juror misconduct](/wiki/Juror_misconduct "Juror misconduct"); the remaining conspiracy count was reduced to a [misdemeanor](/wiki/Misdemeanor "Misdemeanor") and then dismissed.[[210]](#cite_note-213)

A 2002 scheme to underfund pensions for city employees led to the [San Diego pension scandal](/wiki/San_Diego_pension_scandal "San Diego pension scandal"). This resulted in the resignation of newly re-elected Mayor [Dick Murphy](/wiki/Dick_Murphy "Dick Murphy")[[211]](#cite_note-214) and the criminal indictment of six pension board members.[[212]](#cite_note-215) Those charges were finally dismissed by a federal judge in 2010.[[213]](#cite_note-216)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/San_Diego%2C_2016_-_302_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Diego%2C_2016_-_302_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego,_2016_-_302_(cropped).jpg)

[Carter-Keep U.S. Courthouse](/wiki/James_M._Carter_and_Judith_N._Keep_United_States_Courthouse "James M. Carter and Judith N. Keep United States Courthouse")

On November 28, 2005, U.S. Congressman [Randy "Duke" Cunningham](/wiki/Duke_Cunningham "Duke Cunningham") resigned after being convicted on federal [bribery](/wiki/Bribery "Bribery") charges. He had represented [California's 50th congressional district](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives,_California_District_50 "United States House of Representatives, California District 50"), which includes much of the northern portion of the city of San Diego. In 2006, Cunningham was sentenced to a 100-month prison sentence.[[214]](#cite_note-217)

In 2005 two city council members, [Ralph Inzunza](/wiki/Ralph_Inzunza "Ralph Inzunza") and Deputy Mayor [Michael Zucchet](/wiki/Michael_Zucchet "Michael Zucchet"), were convicted of [extortion](/wiki/Extortion "Extortion"), [wire fraud](/wiki/Wire_fraud "Wire fraud"), and [conspiracy to commit wire fraud](/wiki/Conspiracy_(criminal) "Conspiracy (criminal)") for taking campaign contributions from a [strip club](/wiki/Strip_club "Strip club") owner and his associates, allegedly in exchange for trying to repeal the city's "no touch" laws at strip clubs.[[215]](#cite_note-218) Both subsequently resigned. Inzunza was sentenced to 21 months in prison.[[216]](#cite_note-219) In 2009, a judge acquitted Zucchet on seven out of the nine counts against him, and granted his petition for a new trial on the other two charges;[[217]](#cite_note-220) the remaining charges were eventually dropped.[[218]](#cite_note-221)

In July 2013, three former supporters of Mayor [Bob Filner](/wiki/Bob_Filner "Bob Filner") asked him to resign because of allegations of repeated [sexual harassment](/wiki/Sexual_harassment "Sexual harassment").[[219]](#cite_note-222) Over the ensuing six weeks, 18 women came forward to publicly claim that Filner had sexually harassed them,[[220]](#cite_note-223) and multiple individuals and groups called for him to resign. Filner agreed to resign effective August 30, 2013, subsequently pleading guilty to one felony count of [false imprisonment](/wiki/False_imprisonment "False imprisonment") and two misdemeanor [battery](/wiki/Battery_(crime) "Battery (crime)") charges.[[221]](#cite_note-224)[[222]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-225)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Crime")]

Main article: [Crime in San Diego](/wiki/Crime_in_San_Diego "Crime in San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Balboa_Park%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%28129%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Balboa_Park%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%28129%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balboa_Park,_San_Diego,_CA,_USA_-_panoramio_(129)_(cropped).jpg)

[San Diego Police Department](/wiki/San_Diego_Police_Department "San Diego Police Department")

Like most major cities, San Diego had a declining crime rate from 1990 to 2000. 1991 would mark the city's deadliest year, registering 179 homicides[[223]](#cite_note-226) within city limits (while the [region](/wiki/San_Diego_County "San Diego County") as a whole peaked at 278 homicides),[[224]](#cite_note-227) capping off an unabated, eight-year climb in murders, rapes, robberies, and assault dating back to 1983. At the time, the city was ranked last among the 10 most populous U.S. cities in homicides per 1,000 population, and ninth in crimes per 1,000.[[225]](#cite_note-228) From 1980 to 1994, San Diego surpassed 100 murders ten times before tapering off to 91 homicides in 1995. That number would not exceed 79 for the next 15 years.[[226]](#cite_note-229) Crime in San Diego increased in the early 2000s.[[227]](#cite_note-sdpdcrimeactuals-230)[[228]](#cite_note-sdpdcrime1000-231)[[229]](#cite_note-sdtribunecrime-232) In 2004, San Diego had the sixth lowest crime rate of any U.S. city with over half a million residents.[[229]](#cite_note-sdtribunecrime-232) From 2002 to 2006, the crime rate overall dropped 0.8%, though not evenly by category. While [violent crime](/wiki/Violent_crime "Violent crime") decreased 12.4% during this period, [property crime](/wiki/Property_crime "Property crime") increased 1.1%. Total property crimes per 100,000 people were lower than the national average in 2008.[[230]](#cite_note-233)

According to [Uniform Crime Report](/wiki/Uniform_Crime_Report "Uniform Crime Report") statistics compiled by the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") (FBI) in 2010, there were 5,616 violent crimes and 30,753 property crimes. Of these, the violent crimes consisted of forcible rapes, 73 robberies and 170 aggravated assaults, while 6,387 burglaries, 17,977 larceny-thefts, 6,389 motor vehicle thefts and 155 acts of arson defined the property offenses.[[231]](#cite_note-234) In 2013, San Diego had the lowest murder rate of the ten largest cities in the United States.[[232]](#cite_note-235)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

### Primary and secondary schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Primary and secondary schools")]

Main article: [Primary and secondary schools in San Diego](/wiki/Primary_and_secondary_schools_in_San_Diego "Primary and secondary schools in San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/View_across_the_Quad_at_The_Bishop%27s_School_in_La_Jolla_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-View_across_the_Quad_at_The_Bishop%27s_School_in_La_Jolla_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_across_the_Quad_at_The_Bishop%27s_School_in_La_Jolla_(cropped).jpg)

[The Bishop's School](/wiki/The_Bishop%27s_School_(La_Jolla) "The Bishop's School (La Jolla)") in La Jolla

[Public schools](/wiki/State_schools "State schools") in San Diego are operated by independent [school districts](/wiki/School_district "School district"). The majority of the public schools in the city are served by [San Diego Unified School District](/wiki/San_Diego_Unified_School_District "San Diego Unified School District"), the second-largest school district in California, which includes 11 K–8 schools, 107 elementary schools, 24 middle schools, 13 atypical and alternative schools, 28 high schools, and 45 [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school").[[233]](#cite_note-236)

Several adjacent school districts which are headquartered outside the city limits serve some schools within the city; these include [Poway Unified School District](/wiki/Poway_Unified_School_District "Poway Unified School District"), [Del Mar Union School District](/wiki/Del_Mar_Union_School_District "Del Mar Union School District"), [San Dieguito Union High School District](/wiki/San_Dieguito_Union_High_School_District "San Dieguito Union High School District"), and [Sweetwater Union High School District](/wiki/Sweetwater_Union_High_School_District "Sweetwater Union High School District"). In addition, there are a number of private schools in the city.

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/College_West%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%2836%29.jpg/250px-College_West%2C_San_Diego%2C_CA%2C_USA_-_panoramio_%2836%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:College_West,_San_Diego,_CA,_USA_-_panoramio_(36).jpg)

[San Diego State University](/wiki/San_Diego_State_University "San Diego State University")

According to education rankings released by the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") in 2017, 44.4% of San Diegans (city, not county) ages 25 and older hold [bachelor's degrees](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), compared to 30.9% in the United States as a whole.[[234]](#cite_note-237)

The largest university in the area is the [University of California, San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego") (UC San Diego). The university is the southernmost campus of the [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") system and is the second largest employer in the city. It has the seventh largest research expenditure in the country.[[235]](#cite_note-238)

Other public colleges and universities in the city include [San Diego State University](/wiki/San_Diego_State_University "San Diego State University") (SDSU) and the [San Diego Community College District](/wiki/San_Diego_Community_College_District "San Diego Community College District"), which includes [San Diego City College](/wiki/San_Diego_City_College "San Diego City College"), [San Diego Mesa College](/wiki/San_Diego_Mesa_College "San Diego Mesa College"), and [San Diego Miramar College](/wiki/San_Diego_Miramar_College "San Diego Miramar College").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/University_of_San_Diego_%28cropped2%29.jpg/250px-University_of_San_Diego_%28cropped2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_San_Diego_(cropped2).jpg)

[University of San Diego](/wiki/University_of_San_Diego "University of San Diego")

Private non-profit colleges and universities in the city include the [University of San Diego](/wiki/University_of_San_Diego "University of San Diego") (USD), [Point Loma Nazarene University](/wiki/Point_Loma_Nazarene_University "Point Loma Nazarene University") (PLNU), [National University](/wiki/National_University_(California) "National University (California)")'s San Diego campus, [University of Redlands](/wiki/University_of_Redlands "University of Redlands")' School of Business San Diego campus, and [Brandman University](/wiki/Brandman_University "Brandman University")'s San Diego campus. For-profit institutions include [Alliant International University](/wiki/Alliant_International_University "Alliant International University") (AIU), [Fashion Institute of Design & Merchandising](/wiki/Fashion_Institute_of_Design_%26_Merchandising "Fashion Institute of Design & Merchandising")'s San Diego campus, NewSchool of Architecture and Design, [Southern States University](/wiki/Southern_States_University "Southern States University") (SSU), [UEI College](/wiki/UEI_College "UEI College"), and [Woodbury University](/wiki/Woodbury_University "Woodbury University") School of Architecture's satellite campus.

There is one medical school in the city, the [UC San Diego School of Medicine](/wiki/UC_San_Diego_School_of_Medicine "UC San Diego School of Medicine"). There are two [ABA](/wiki/American_Bar_Association "American Bar Association") accredited law schools in the city, which include [California Western School of Law](/wiki/California_Western_School_of_Law "California Western School of Law") and [University of San Diego School of Law](/wiki/University_of_San_Diego_School_of_Law "University of San Diego School of Law"). There are also two law schools, [Thomas Jefferson School of Law](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_School_of_Law "Thomas Jefferson School of Law") and [Western Sierra Law School](/wiki/Western_Sierra_Law_School "Western Sierra Law School"), not accredited by the ABA.

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Geisel_Library3.jpg/250px-Geisel_Library3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geisel_Library3.jpg)

[Geisel Library](/wiki/Geisel_Library "Geisel Library") at [UC San Diego](/wiki/UC_San_Diego "UC San Diego")

The city-run [San Diego Public Library](/wiki/San_Diego_Public_Library "San Diego Public Library") system is headquartered downtown and has 36 branches throughout the city.[[236]](#cite_note-239) The newest location is in Skyline Hills, which broke ground in 2015.[[237]](#cite_note-240) The libraries have had reduced operating hours since 2003 due to the city's financial problems. In 2006 the city increased spending on libraries by $2.1 million.[[238]](#cite_note-SpareLibPark-241) A new nine-story Central Library on Park Boulevard at J Street opened on September 30, 2013.[[239]](#cite_note-242)

In addition to the municipal public library system, there are nearly two dozen libraries open to the public run by other governmental agencies, and by schools, colleges, and universities.[[240]](#cite_note-243) Noteworthy are [Malcolm A. Love Library](/wiki/Malcolm_A._Love_Library "Malcolm A. Love Library") at [San Diego State University](/wiki/San_Diego_State_University "San Diego State University"), and [Geisel Library](/wiki/Geisel_Library "Geisel Library") at the [University of California, San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego").

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of San Diego](/wiki/Culture_of_San_Diego "Culture of San Diego")

See also: [City of San Diego Commission for Arts and Culture](/wiki/City_of_San_Diego_Commission_for_Arts_and_Culture "City of San Diego Commission for Arts and Culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/San_Diego_Museum_of_Man_01_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Diego_Museum_of_Man_01_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego_Museum_of_Man_01_(cropped).jpg)

The [Museum of Us](/wiki/Museum_of_Us "Museum of Us")

The culture of San Diego is influenced heavily by the mixing of [American](/wiki/American_culture "American culture") and [Mexican cultures](/wiki/Mexican_culture "Mexican culture"), due to the city's position on the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"), its large [Chicano](/wiki/Chicano "Chicano") population, and its history as part of [Hispanic America](/wiki/Hispanic_America "Hispanic America") and Mexico. San Diego's longtime association with the [U.S. military](/wiki/U.S._military "U.S. military") also contributes to its culture. There are many [Greek](/wiki/Greek_cuisine "Greek cuisine") and Mediterranean restaurants in San Diego.

Many popular museums, such as [the San Diego Museum of Art](/wiki/The_San_Diego_Museum_of_Art "The San Diego Museum of Art"), the [San Diego Natural History Museum](/wiki/San_Diego_Natural_History_Museum "San Diego Natural History Museum"), the [Museum of Us](/wiki/Museum_of_Us "Museum of Us"), the [Museum of Photographic Arts](/wiki/Museum_of_Photographic_Arts "Museum of Photographic Arts"), and the [San Diego Air & Space Museum](/wiki/San_Diego_Air_%26_Space_Museum "San Diego Air & Space Museum"), are located in Balboa Park, which is also the location of the [San Diego Zoo](/wiki/San_Diego_Zoo "San Diego Zoo"). The [Museum of Contemporary Art San Diego](/wiki/Museum_of_Contemporary_Art_San_Diego "Museum of Contemporary Art San Diego") (MCASD) is located in La Jolla and has a branch located at [Santa Fe Depot](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Depot_(San_Diego) "Santa Fe Depot (San Diego)") downtown.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/San_Diego_Museum_of_Art_02.jpg/250px-San_Diego_Museum_of_Art_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego_Museum_of_Art_02.jpg)

[The San Diego Museum of Art](/wiki/The_San_Diego_Museum_of_Art "The San Diego Museum of Art")

The [Columbia district](/wiki/Columbia,_San_Diego,_California "Columbia, San Diego, California") downtown is home to historic ship exhibits belonging to the [Maritime Museum of San Diego](/wiki/Maritime_Museum_of_San_Diego "Maritime Museum of San Diego"), headlined by [*Star of India*](/wiki/Star_of_India_(ship) "Star of India (ship)"), as well as the unrelated [USS *Midway* Museum](/wiki/USS_Midway_Museum "USS Midway Museum") featuring the [USS *Midway*](/wiki/USS_Midway_(CV-41) "USS Midway (CV-41)") aircraft carrier.

The [San Diego Symphony](/wiki/San_Diego_Symphony "San Diego Symphony") at [Symphony Towers](/wiki/Symphony_Towers "Symphony Towers") performs on a regular basis; from 2004 to 2017, its director was [Jahja Ling](/wiki/Jahja_Ling "Jahja Ling"). The [San Diego Opera](/wiki/San_Diego_Opera "San Diego Opera") at Civic Center Plaza, directed by David Bennett. [Old Globe Theatre](/wiki/Old_Globe_Theatre "Old Globe Theatre") at Balboa Park produces about 15 plays and musicals annually. [La Jolla Playhouse](/wiki/La_Jolla_Playhouse "La Jolla Playhouse") at UC San Diego is directed by [Christopher Ashley](/wiki/Christopher_Ashley "Christopher Ashley"). Both the Old Globe Theatre and La Jolla Playhouse have produced the world premieres of plays and musicals that have gone on to win [Tony Awards](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards")[[241]](#cite_note-244) or nominations[[242]](#cite_note-245) on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre"). The [Joan B. Kroc](/wiki/Joan_Kroc "Joan Kroc") Theatre at Kroc Center's Performing Arts Center is a 600-seat state-of-the-art theater that hosts music, dance, and theater performances. Hundreds of movies and a dozen TV shows have been [filmed in San Diego](/wiki/List_of_media_set_in_San_Diego#Films "List of media set in San Diego"), a tradition going back as far as 1898.[[243]](#cite_note-246)

### LGBTQIA+ community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: LGBTQIA+ community")]

According to a 2021 report by the UCLA School of Law, San Diego has the 16th largest LGBT population amongst U.S. Metropolitan areas.[[244]](#cite_note-247) San Diego's [Hillcrest](/wiki/Hillcrest,_San_Diego "Hillcrest, San Diego") neighborhood has long served as the city's [gay village](/wiki/Gay_village "Gay village") and is home to The San Diego LGBT Community Center, as well as many gay bars, clubs, and queer-owned businesses. The restaurant and nightclub Gossip Grill, located in Hillcrest, is San Diego's only queer and women-focused venue and is notable as one of the country's last remaining "[lesbian bars](/wiki/Lesbian_bar "Lesbian bar")."[[245]](#cite_note-248)[[246]](#cite_note-249) The nearby neighborhood of Northpark is the location of Lips, a drag club and home base of RuPaul's Drag Race All Stars (Season 1) winner [Chad Michaels](/wiki/Chad_Michaels "Chad Michaels").[[247]](#cite_note-250)

The Hillcrest neighborhood is also home to the Lambda Archives of San Diego, a non-profit, publicly accessible archive that collects, preserves, and shares the history of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender and all queer people in the region.[[248]](#cite_note-251)

The city hosts a [Pride Parade and Pride Festival](/wiki/San_Diego_Pride "San Diego Pride") each year. Unlike most other cities, San Diego celebrates Pride in July, a tradition dating back to the early 1990s, when the city's "June gloom" weather led to the 1990 parade getting rained out.[[249]](#cite_note-252)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sports in San Diego](/wiki/Sports_in_San_Diego "Sports in San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Petco_Park_Padres_Game.jpg/250px-Petco_Park_Padres_Game.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petco_Park_Padres_Game.jpg)

[Petco Park](/wiki/Petco_Park "Petco Park"), home of the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") (MLB)

Sports in San Diego includes [major professional league](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") teams, other [highest-level professional league](/wiki/Professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States#Other_highest-level_professional_leagues "Professional sports leagues in the United States") teams, [minor league](/wiki/Minor_league "Minor league") teams, and [college athletics](/wiki/College_athletics "College athletics"). San Diego hosts three teams of major professional leagues, the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB), [San Diego FC](/wiki/San_Diego_FC "San Diego FC") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS), and [San Diego Wave FC](/wiki/San_Diego_Wave_FC "San Diego Wave FC") of [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") (NWSL).[[250]](#cite_note-MLS_Announcement2-253) The city is home to several universities whose teams compete in various [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") sports, most notably the [San Diego State Aztecs](/wiki/San_Diego_State_Aztecs "San Diego State Aztecs"). The [Farmers Insurance Open](/wiki/Farmers_Insurance_Open "Farmers Insurance Open"), a professional golf tournament on the [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour"), is played annually at [Torrey Pines Golf Course](/wiki/Torrey_Pines_Golf_Course "Torrey Pines Golf Course").

San Diego hosted the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL)'s [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") from 1961 to 2017, when the team relocated to the [Greater Los Angeles](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles") area (now the [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers")). The city also hosted the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA)'s [San Diego Rockets](/wiki/San_Diego_Rockets "San Diego Rockets") from 1967 to 1971 (now the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets")) and [San Diego Clippers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Clippers#1978–1984:_San_Diego_Clippers "Los Angeles Clippers") from 1978 to 1984 (now the [Los Angeles Clippers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Clippers "Los Angeles Clippers")). San Diego has never hosted a [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) franchise, though it hosted the [San Diego Mariners](/wiki/San_Diego_Mariners "San Diego Mariners") of the now-defunct [World Hockey Association](/wiki/World_Hockey_Association "World Hockey Association") (WHA) from 1974 to 1977.

Currently, there is no NBA, NFL, or NHL team in the city. San Diego is the largest American city not to have won a championship in a "Big Four"[[a]](#cite_note-254) major professional league. The city does have one major league title to its name: the [1963 American Football League (AFL) Championship](/wiki/1963_American_Football_League_Championship_Game "1963 American Football League Championship Game") won by the San Diego Chargers, when the AFL was an independent entity prior to the [AFL–NFL merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") in 1970. Some San Diego sports fans believe there is [a curse](/wiki/Sports-related_curses#San_Diego,_California "Sports-related curses") on professional sports in the city.

The [San Diego Clippers](/wiki/San_Diego_Clippers_(NBA_G_League) "San Diego Clippers (NBA G League)") of the [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League") have played at [Frontwave Arena](/wiki/Frontwave_Arena "Frontwave Arena") in the suburb of [Oceanside](/wiki/Oceanside,_California "Oceanside, California") since 2024. [Pechanga Arena](/wiki/Pechanga_Arena "Pechanga Arena") hosts The [San Diego Gulls](/wiki/San_Diego_Gulls "San Diego Gulls") of the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"), and The [San Diego Seals](/wiki/San_Diego_Seals "San Diego Seals") of the [National Lacrosse League](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League").

In March 2026, it was announced that San Diego's [Snapdragon Stadium](/wiki/Snapdragon_Stadium "Snapdragon Stadium") will host 11 matches during the [LA28](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics") Olympic Football (Soccer) Tournament in July 2028.[[251]](#cite_note-255)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Media")]

See also: [Media in San Diego](/wiki/Media_in_San_Diego "Media in San Diego") and [List of media set in San Diego](/wiki/List_of_media_set_in_San_Diego "List of media set in San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/San_Pasqual_Battle_Monument.png/250px-San_Pasqual_Battle_Monument.png)](/wiki/File:San_Pasqual_Battle_Monument.png)

The [San Pasqual Battlefield Monument](/wiki/San_Pasqual_Battlefield_Monument "San Pasqual Battlefield Monument") commemorates the 1846 [Battle of San Pasqual](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Pasqual "Battle of San Pasqual").

Published within the city are the daily newspaper, *[The San Diego Union-Tribune](/wiki/The_San_Diego_Union-Tribune "The San Diego Union-Tribune")* and its online portal of the same name,[[252]](#cite_note-256) and the alternative newsweeklies, *[San Diego CityBeat](/wiki/San_Diego_CityBeat "San Diego CityBeat")* and the *[San Diego Reader](/wiki/San_Diego_Reader "San Diego Reader")*. The *[Times of San Diego](/wiki/Times_of_San_Diego "Times of San Diego")* is a free online newspaper covering news in the metropolitan area. *[Voice of San Diego](/wiki/Voice_of_San_Diego "Voice of San Diego")* is a non-profit online news outlet covering government, politics, education, neighborhoods, and the arts. The *San Diego Daily Transcript* is a business-oriented online newspaper. San Diego is also the headquarters of the national [far-right](/wiki/Far-right_politics_in_the_United_States "Far-right politics in the United States") cable TV channel [One America News Network](/wiki/One_America_News_Network "One America News Network") (OANN).

San Diego led U.S. local markets with 69.6 percent broadband penetration in 2004 according to [Nielsen//NetRatings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings").[[253]](#cite_note-257)

San Diego's first television station was [KFMB](/wiki/KFMB-TV "KFMB-TV"), which began broadcasting on May 16, 1949.[[254]](#cite_note-KFMBTV-258) Since the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) licensed seven television stations in Los Angeles, two [VHF](/wiki/VHF "VHF") channels were available for San Diego because of its relative proximity to the larger city. In 1952, however, the FCC began licensing [UHF](/wiki/UHF "UHF") channels, making it possible for cities such as San Diego to acquire more stations. Stations based in Mexico (with [ITU prefixes](/wiki/ITU_prefix "ITU prefix") of XE and XH) also serve the San Diego market. Television stations today include [XHJK-TDT](/wiki/XHJK-TDT "XHJK-TDT") 1 ([Azteca Uno](/wiki/Azteca_Uno "Azteca Uno")), [XETV-TDT](/wiki/XETV-TDT "XETV-TDT") 6 ([Canal 5](/wiki/Canal_5_(Mexico) "Canal 5 (Mexico)")/[Nueve](/wiki/Nueve_(Mexican_TV_network) "Nueve (Mexican TV network)")), [KFMB](/wiki/KFMB-TV "KFMB-TV") 8 ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), with [MNTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV") on DT2), [KGTV](/wiki/KGTV "KGTV") 10 ([ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")), [XHCPDE](/wiki/XHCPDE-TDT "XHCPDE-TDT") 11 ([Once](/wiki/Canal_Once_(Mexico) "Canal Once (Mexico)")), [XEWT](/wiki/XEWT-TDT "XEWT-TDT") 12 ([Televisa Regional](/wiki/Televisa_Regional "Televisa Regional")), [KPBS](/wiki/KPBS_(TV) "KPBS (TV)") 15 ([PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS")), [KBNT-CD](/wiki/KBNT-CD "KBNT-CD") 17 ([Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision")), [XHTIT-TDT](/wiki/XHTIT-TDT "XHTIT-TDT") 21 ([Azteca 7](/wiki/Azteca_7 "Azteca 7")), [XHAS-TDT](/wiki/XHAS-TDT "XHAS-TDT") 33 ([Canal 66](/wiki/XHILA-TDT "XHILA-TDT")), [KDTF-LD](/wiki/KDTF-LD "KDTF-LD") 36 ([Unimás](/wiki/Unim%C3%A1s "Unimás")), [KNSD](/wiki/KNSD "KNSD") 39 ([NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")), [KSKT-CD](/wiki/KSKT-CD "KSKT-CD") 43 ([Estrella TV](/wiki/Estrella_TV "Estrella TV")), [XHBJ-TDT](/wiki/XHBJ-TDT "XHBJ-TDT") 45 (Canal 45 PSN), [KUAN-LD](/wiki/KUAN-LD "KUAN-LD") 48 ([Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo")), [XHDTV-TDT](/wiki/XHDTV-TDT "XHDTV-TDT") 49 ([Milenio Televisión](/wiki/Milenio_Televisi%C3%B3n "Milenio Televisión")), [KUSI](/wiki/KUSI "KUSI") 51 ([The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW")), [XHUAA-TDT](/wiki/XHUAA-TDT "XHUAA-TDT") 57 ([El Canal de las Estrellas](/wiki/El_Canal_de_las_Estrellas "El Canal de las Estrellas")), and [KSWB-TV](/wiki/KSWB-TV "KSWB-TV") 69 ([Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")). San Diego has an 80.6 percent cable penetration rate.[[255]](#cite_note-San_Diego_cable_penetration_rates-259)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Parade_of_Lights_2017_15_-_39114099902.jpg/250px-Parade_of_Lights_2017_15_-_39114099902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parade_of_Lights_2017_15_-_39114099902.jpg)

San Diego Parade of Lights

Due to the ratio of U.S. and Mexican-licensed stations, San Diego is the largest [media market](/wiki/Media_market "Media market") in the United States that is legally unable to support a [television station duopoly](/wiki/Duopoly_(broadcasting) "Duopoly (broadcasting)") between two full-power stations under [FCC](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") regulations, which disallow duopolies in metropolitan areas with fewer than nine full-power television stations and require that there would be eight unique station owners that remain once a duopoly is formed (there are only seven full-power stations on the California side of the San Diego-Tijuana market).[[256]](#cite_note-260) As a whole, the Mexico side of the San Diego-Tijuana market has two duopolies and one triopoly ([Entravision Communications](/wiki/Entravision_Communications "Entravision Communications") owns XHDTV-TV, [Azteca](/wiki/Azteca_(multimedia_company) "Azteca (multimedia company)") owns [XHJK-TV](/wiki/XHJK-TV "XHJK-TV") and [XHTIT-TV](/wiki/XHTIT-TV "XHTIT-TV"), and [Grupo Televisa](/wiki/Televisa "Televisa") owns [XETV-TV](/wiki/XETV-TDT "XETV-TDT"), [XHUAA-TV](/wiki/XHUAA-TV "XHUAA-TV") and [XEWT-TV](/wiki/XEWT-TDT "XEWT-TDT").

San Diego's television market is limited to only [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County "San Diego County"). As a result, San Diego is the largest single-county media market in the United States.

The radio stations in San Diego include nationwide broadcaster [iHeartMedia](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia"), [Audacy, Inc.](/wiki/Audacy,_Inc. "Audacy, Inc."), Local Media San Diego, and many other smaller stations and networks. Stations include: [KOGO AM 600](/wiki/KOGO_(AM) "KOGO (AM)"), [KGB AM 760](/wiki/KGB_(AM) "KGB (AM)"), [KCEO AM 1000](/wiki/KCEO "KCEO"), [KCBQ AM 1170](/wiki/KCBQ "KCBQ"), [K-Praise](/wiki/KPRZ "KPRZ"), [KLSD AM 1360](/wiki/KLSD "KLSD"), [KFSD 1450 AM](/wiki/KFSD "KFSD"), [KPBS-FM](/wiki/KPBS-FM "KPBS-FM") 89.5, [Channel 933](/wiki/KHTS-FM "KHTS-FM"), [Star 94.1](/wiki/KMYI "KMYI"), [FM 94/9](/wiki/KBZT "KBZT"), [FM News and Talk 95.7](/wiki/KSSX "KSSX"), [Q96](/wiki/KYDO "KYDO") 96.1, [KyXy](/wiki/KYXY "KYXY") 96.5, [Free Radio San Diego](/wiki/Free_Radio_San_Diego "Free Radio San Diego") (AKA [Pirate Radio](/wiki/Pirate_Radio "Pirate Radio") San Diego) 96.9FM FRSD, [KWFN](/wiki/KWFN "KWFN") 97.3, [KXSN](/wiki/KXSN "KXSN") 98.1, [Frontera FM 100.7](/wiki/KFBG_(FM) "KFBG (FM)"), 101.5 [KGB-FM](/wiki/KGB-FM "KGB-FM"), [KLVJ](/wiki/KLVJ_(FM) "KLVJ (FM)") 102.1, [KSON](/wiki/KSON_(FM) "KSON (FM)") 103.7, [Rock 105.3](/wiki/KIOZ "KIOZ"), and another *[Pirate Radio](/wiki/Pirate_Radio "Pirate Radio")* station at 106.9FM, as well as a number of local Spanish-language radio stations.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main articles: [Transportation in San Diego](/wiki/Transportation_in_San_Diego "Transportation in San Diego") and [Streets and highways of San Diego](/wiki/Streets_and_highways_of_San_Diego "Streets and highways of San Diego")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Union_Station%2C_San_Diego_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Union_Station%2C_San_Diego_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Station,_San_Diego_(cropped).jpg)

[Santa Fe Depot](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Depot_(San_Diego) "Santa Fe Depot (San Diego)") is served by [Amtrak California](/wiki/Amtrak_California "Amtrak California") and [Coaster](/wiki/Coaster_(rail_service) "Coaster (rail service)") trains.

With the automobile being the primary means of transportation for over 80 percent of residents, San Diego is served by a network of freeways and highways. This includes [Interstate 5](/wiki/Interstate_5_in_California "Interstate 5 in California"), which runs south to [Tijuana](/wiki/Tijuana "Tijuana") and north to Los Angeles; [Interstate 8](/wiki/Interstate_8 "Interstate 8"), which runs east to [Imperial County](/wiki/Imperial_County,_California "Imperial County, California") and the [Arizona Sun Corridor](/wiki/Arizona_Sun_Corridor "Arizona Sun Corridor"); Interstate 15, which runs northeast through the [Inland Empire](/wiki/Inland_Empire "Inland Empire") to [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") and [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"); and [Interstate 805](/wiki/Interstate_805 "Interstate 805"), which splits from I-5 near the Mexican border and rejoins I-5 at [Sorrento Valley](/wiki/Sorrento_Valley,_San_Diego "Sorrento Valley, San Diego").

Major state highways include [SR 94](/wiki/California_State_Route_94 "California State Route 94"), which connects downtown with I-805, I-15 and [East County](/wiki/East_County,_San_Diego "East County, San Diego"); [SR 163](/wiki/California_State_Route_163 "California State Route 163"), which connects downtown with the northeast part of the city, intersects I-805 and merges with I-15 at [Miramar](/wiki/Miramar,_San_Diego "Miramar, San Diego"); [SR 52](/wiki/California_State_Route_52 "California State Route 52"), which connects La Jolla with [East County](/wiki/East_County,_San_Diego "East County, San Diego") through [Santee](/wiki/Santee,_California "Santee, California") and [SR 125](/wiki/California_State_Route_125 "California State Route 125"); [SR 56](/wiki/California_State_Route_56 "California State Route 56"), which connects I-5 with I-15 through [Carmel Valley](/wiki/Carmel_Valley,_San_Diego "Carmel Valley, San Diego") and [Rancho Peñasquitos](/wiki/Rancho_Pe%C3%B1asquitos,_San_Diego "Rancho Peñasquitos, San Diego"); [SR 75](/wiki/California_State_Route_75 "California State Route 75"), which spans [San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay") as the [San Diego–Coronado Bridge](/wiki/San_Diego%E2%80%93Coronado_Bridge "San Diego–Coronado Bridge"), and also passes through [South San Diego](/wiki/South_San_Diego "South San Diego") as Palm Avenue; and [SR 905](/wiki/California_State_Route_905 "California State Route 905"), which connects I-5 and I-805 to the [Otay Mesa Port of Entry](/wiki/Otay_Mesa_Port_of_Entry "Otay Mesa Port of Entry").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/MTS_Blue_Line_Siemens_S700_US_Santa_Fe_Depot_%2852227314521%29.jpg/250px-MTS_Blue_Line_Siemens_S700_US_Santa_Fe_Depot_%2852227314521%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MTS_Blue_Line_Siemens_S700_US_Santa_Fe_Depot_(52227314521).jpg)

[San Diego Trolley](/wiki/San_Diego_Trolley "San Diego Trolley") is operated by the [S.D. Metropolitan Transit System](/wiki/San_Diego_Metropolitan_Transit_System "San Diego Metropolitan Transit System").

The stretch of SR 163 that passes through Balboa Park is San Diego's oldest freeway, dating back to 1948 when it was part of [US 80](/wiki/U.S._Route_80_in_California "U.S. Route 80 in California") and [US 395](/wiki/U.S._Route_395_in_California "U.S. Route 395 in California"). It has been called one of America's most beautiful parkways.[[257]](#cite_note-261)

San Diego's roadway system provides an extensive network of cycle routes. Its dry and mild climate makes cycling a convenient year-round option; however, the city's hilly terrain and long average trip distances make cycling less practicable. Older and denser neighborhoods around the downtown tend to be oriented to [utility cycling](/wiki/Utility_cycling "Utility cycling"). This is partly because the grid street patterns are now absent in newer developments farther from the urban core, where suburban-style arterial roads are much more common. As a result, the majority of cycling is recreational.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/CBX_exterior.jpg/250px-CBX_exterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:CBX_exterior.jpg)

The [Cross Border Xpress](/wiki/Cross_Border_Xpress "Cross Border Xpress"), also known as the *Puerta de [las Californias](/wiki/Las_Californias "Las Californias")*, connects San Diego to [Tijuana International Airport](/wiki/Tijuana_International_Airport "Tijuana International Airport") in [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California").

San Diego is served by the [San Diego Trolley](/wiki/San_Diego_Trolley "San Diego Trolley") light rail system,[[258]](#cite_note-SD-Trolley-262) by the [MTS bus system](/wiki/San_Diego_MTS_bus_system "San Diego MTS bus system"),[[259]](#cite_note-263) the bus rapid transit system [Rapid](/wiki/Rapid_(San_Diego) "Rapid (San Diego)"), private [jitneys](/wiki/Share_taxi#United_States "Share taxi") in some neighborhoods,[[260]](#cite_note-264) and by [Coaster](/wiki/Coaster_(rail_service) "Coaster (rail service)")[[261]](#cite_note-Coaster-265) and *[Pacific Surfliner](/wiki/Pacific_Surfliner "Pacific Surfliner")*[[262]](#cite_note-Surfliner-266) commuter rail; northern [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County,_California "San Diego County, California") is also served by the [Sprinter](/wiki/Sprinter_(rail_service) "Sprinter (rail service)") hybrid rail service.[[263]](#cite_note-267) The trolley primarily serves downtown and surrounding urban communities, [Mission Valley](/wiki/Mission_Valley,_San_Diego,_California "Mission Valley, San Diego, California"), east county, and coastal south bay. A mid-coast extension of the trolley operates from [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town,_San_Diego,_California "Old Town, San Diego, California") to [University City](/wiki/University_City,_San_Diego,_California "University City, San Diego, California") and the [University of California, San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego") along [Interstate 5](/wiki/Interstate_5 "Interstate 5") since November 2021. The Amtrak and Coaster trains currently run along the coastline and connect San Diego with Los Angeles, Orange County, Riverside, San Bernardino, and Ventura via [Metrolink](/wiki/Metrolink_(California) "Metrolink (California)") and the Pacific Surfliner. There are two Amtrak stations in San Diego, in [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town_Transit_Center "Old Town Transit Center") and [Santa Fe Depot](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Depot_(San_Diego) "Santa Fe Depot (San Diego)") downtown. San Diego transit information about public transportation and commuting is available on the Web and by dialing "[511](/wiki/5-1-1 "5-1-1")" from any phone in the area.[[264]](#cite_note-268)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/San_Diego_International_Airport_%28KSAN%29_Terminal_2_%28upper_deck%29_-_August_2018.jpg/250px-San_Diego_International_Airport_%28KSAN%29_Terminal_2_%28upper_deck%29_-_August_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego_International_Airport_(KSAN)_Terminal_2_(upper_deck)_-_August_2018.jpg)

[San Diego International Airport](/wiki/San_Diego_International_Airport "San Diego International Airport")

The city has two major commercial airports within or near its city limits. [San Diego International Airport](/wiki/San_Diego_International_Airport "San Diego International Airport") (SAN) is the busiest single-runway airport in the United States.[[265]](#cite_note-FAAresign-269)[[266]](#cite_note-270) It served over 24 million passengers in 2018 and is dealing with larger numbers every year.[[267]](#cite_note-271) [Tijuana International Airport](/wiki/Tijuana_International_Airport "Tijuana International Airport") has a terminal within the city limits in the [Otay Mesa](/wiki/Otay_Mesa "Otay Mesa") district connected to the rest of the airport in [Tijuana, Mexico](/wiki/Tijuana,_Mexico "Tijuana, Mexico"), via the [Cross Border Xpress](/wiki/Cross_Border_Xpress "Cross Border Xpress") cross-border footbridge. In addition, the city has two general-aviation airports, [Montgomery-Gibbs Executive Airport](/wiki/Montgomery-Gibbs_Executive_Airport "Montgomery-Gibbs Executive Airport") (MYF) and [Brown Field Municipal Airport](/wiki/Brown_Field_Municipal_Airport "Brown Field Municipal Airport") (SDM).[[268]](#cite_note-272)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/2012_Festival_of_Sail_934278934983.jpg/250px-2012_Festival_of_Sail_934278934983.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012_Festival_of_Sail_934278934983.jpg)

[San Diego Bay](/wiki/San_Diego_Bay "San Diego Bay") Festival of Sail

Recent regional transportation projects have sought to mitigate congestion, including improvements to local freeways, expansion of San Diego Airport, and doubling the capacity of the cruise ship terminal. Freeway projects included expansion of Interstates 5 and 805 around "The Merge" where these two freeways meet, as well as expansion of Interstate 15 through North County, which includes new [HOV "managed lanes"](/wiki/High-occupancy_vehicle_lane "High-occupancy vehicle lane"). A tollway (the southern portion of SR 125, known as the South Bay Expressway) connects SR 54 and Otay Mesa, near the Mexican border. According to an assessment in 2007, 37 percent of city streets were in acceptable condition. However, the proposed budget fell $84.6 million short of bringing streets up to an acceptable level.[[269]](#cite_note-37perStreets-273) Expansion at the port has included a second cruise terminal on [Broadway Pier](/wiki/Broadway_Pier,_San_Diego "Broadway Pier, San Diego"), opened in 2010. Airport projects include the expansion of Terminal Two.[[270]](#cite_note-274)

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Utilities")]

Water is supplied to residents by the Water Department of the City of San Diego. The city receives most of its water from the [Metropolitan Water District of Southern California](/wiki/Metropolitan_Water_District_of_Southern_California "Metropolitan Water District of Southern California"), which brings water to the region from the Sacramento and San Joaquin rivers, via the state project and the Colorado River, via the Colorado Aqueduct.[[271]](#cite_note-275) 80–90% of the water supply is imported.[[272]](#cite_note-276)

Gas and electric utilities are provided by [San Diego Gas & Electric](/wiki/San_Diego_Gas_%26_Electric "San Diego Gas & Electric"), a division of [Sempra Energy](/wiki/Sempra_Energy "Sempra Energy").[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The company provides energy service to 3.7 million people through 1.5 million electric meters and 900,000 natural gas meters in San Diego and southern Orange counties.[[273]](#cite_note-277)

#### Street lights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Street lights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Sandiego_gaslampquarter_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Sandiego_gaslampquarter_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sandiego_gaslampquarter_(cropped).JPG)

Street lights in the [Gaslamp Quarter](/wiki/Gaslamp_Quarter "Gaslamp Quarter")

In the mid-20th century the city had [mercury vapor](/wiki/Mercury_vapor "Mercury vapor") street lamps. In 1978, the city decided to replace them with more efficient [sodium vapor](/wiki/Sodium_vapor "Sodium vapor") lamps. This triggered an outcry from [astronomers](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") at [Palomar Observatory](/wiki/Palomar_Observatory "Palomar Observatory") 60 miles (100 km) north of the city, concerned that the new lamps would increase [light pollution](/wiki/Light_pollution "Light pollution") and hinder astronomical observation.[[274]](#cite_note-278) The city altered its lighting regulations to limit light pollution within 30 miles (50 km) of Palomar.[[275]](#cite_note-279)

In 2011, the city announced plans to upgrade 80% of its street lighting to new energy-efficient lights that use [induction technology](/wiki/Electrodeless_lamp "Electrodeless lamp"), a modified form of [fluorescent lamp](/wiki/Fluorescent_lamp "Fluorescent lamp") producing a broader spectrum than sodium vapor lamps. The new system is predicted to save $2.2 million per year in energy and maintenance.[[276]](#cite_note-install-280) In 2014, San Diego announced plans to become the first U.S. city to install cyber-controlled street lighting.[[277]](#cite_note-281)
